Lab 2 — Visualizing Ecuador's Export Time Series¶

Course: Visualization Analysis & Design Topic: Time Series Visualization & Storytelling with Data — applied to Ecuador's foreign trade

Overview¶

Time series data is everywhere, and few datasets tell a richer story than a country's export revenue. In this lab we use real monthly export data from the Banco Central del Ecuador (BCE) — all primary and industrialized products — to practice the full toolbox of time-series visualization, and then to learn how to turn a chart into a story that drives a decision.

This lab walks you through the concepts from the Week 2 lecture:

  1. What is a time series? — definition, components, and decomposition.
  2. Infovis taxonomies — shared-space, split-space, and space-filling layouts.
  3. Visual methods — heatmaps, calendar views, stacked/stream graphs, scatter & box plots, Sankey diagrams, and parallel coordinates.
  4. Interactive techniques — tooltips, zoom, filter, range sliders, and a multi-view dashboard.
  5. Storytelling with data — Knaflic's (2015) six lessons applied to Ecuador's export diversification.

Grading¶

Each activity is worth the same number of points.

Dataset¶

We use the "Exportaciones FOB por Producto Principal" table from the BCE's Información Estadística Mensual (IEM), file IEM-311-e.xlsx, downloaded automatically from the course's public dataset repository:

https://github.com/CarlaGeovanna/datasets_public/blob/main/IEM-311-e.xlsx

The file provides 34 months of real monthly data (January 2023 – October 2025) covering all export products the BCE tracks:

Group Products
Primary Petroleum, Bananas, Coffee, Shrimp, Cocoa, Abacá, Timber, Tuna, Fish, Flowers, Other Primary (11)
Industrialized Petroleum Derivatives, Processed Coffee, Processed Cocoa, Fish Meal, Other Sea Products, Chemicals, Metal Manufactures, Hats, Textiles, Other Industrialized (10)

Values are reported in thousands of USD (Miles de USD) and converted to millions of USD for readability.

Setup¶

  • The cell below installs Plotly and openpyxl (needed to read .xlsx files on Colab). Everything else (pandas, numpy, matplotlib, seaborn) is pre-installed.

  • Plotly — makes interactive charts (hover, zoom, filter) directly in the notebook. Used for the Sankey diagram, parallel coordinates, and the interactive time series explorer.

  • Openpyxl — lets Python read and write .xlsx files (Excel format). Used here so pandas can open the BCE file IEM-311-e.xlsx that is downloaded from GitHub.

In [1]:
# Plotly and openpyxl are not pre-installed on Colab
!pip install plotly openpyxl --quiet
  • The imports cell below configures the Plotly renderer automatically — correct whether you run in Colab browser, VS Code + Colab plugin, or local Jupyter. After it runs, always call fig.show() with no renderer= argument.
In [2]:
import numpy as np                         # numerical arrays and math operations
import pandas as pd                        # DataFrames and time series handling
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt            # base static plotting library
import matplotlib.dates as mdates          # date formatting utilities for axes
import seaborn as sns                      # statistical heatmaps
import plotly.express as px                # high-level interactive charts
import plotly.graph_objects as go          # low-level interactive chart objects
import plotly.io as pio                    # renderer configuration
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots  # multi-panel Plotly figures
import os, sys, pathlib, urllib.request    # file system and download utilities

# Auto-detect which environment is running so Plotly renders correctly.
# JPY_SESSION_NAME starts with /content/ only when using VS Code + Colab plugin.
_jpy = os.environ.get('JPY_SESSION_NAME', '')
if _jpy.startswith('/content/'):
    pio.renderers.default = 'vscode'   # VS Code + Colab plugin
elif 'google.colab' in sys.modules:
    pio.renderers.default = 'colab'    # Colab in the browser
else:
    pio.renderers.default = 'notebook' # local Jupyter fallback

print(f"Using Plotly renderer: {pio.renderers.default}")
print("All libraries loaded successfully.")

Using Plotly renderer: vscode
All libraries loaded successfully.

Part 1 — What Is a Time Series?¶

1.1 Definition and Mathematical Representation¶

A time series is a sequence of observations collected from the same entity at regular (or irregular) time intervals. Formally:

$$D = \{(t_1, v_1),\ (t_2, v_2),\ \ldots,\ (t_n, v_n)\}$$

where $t_i$ is the instant of time and $v_i$ is the value observed at that moment.

The value $v_i$ can be a scalar (e.g., monthly petroleum exports → unidimensional) or a vector (e.g., all 21 export products → multidimensional time series). When we stack multiple products, the result is a data cube: each month is one time slice, each product is one dimension.

Time series data is used for:

  • Analyzing the past — which products drove Ecuador's export growth in 2024?
  • Forecasting — projecting shrimp revenue for the next budget cycle.
  • Informing decisions — trade policy, diversification, infrastructure investment.

1.2 The Four Components of a Time Series¶

Component Description Example in Ecuador's exports
Trend Long-term direction Cocoa growing throughout 2023–2025
Seasonality Pattern repeating at fixed intervals Petroleum higher in September–October
Cycle Recurring but non-seasonal fluctuation Multi-year commodity price cycles
Irregular / Noise Unpredictable variation Month-to-month demand shocks

With 34 months of real monthly data (two complete annual cycles: 2023 and 2024, plus January–October 2025), genuine trend and seasonal patterns are observable in the data.

Loading the complete BCE dataset¶

The cell below downloads IEM-311-e.xlsx and parses all 21 export products into two DataFrames:

  • df_monthly — 34 real monthly rows (Jan 2023 – Oct 2025). Primary dataset for all visualizations.
  • df_annual — 5 annual rows (2020–2024). Used for long-term storytelling and the Sankey diagram.

Three aggregate columns are also computed per row:

  • Total_Primary — sum of the 11 primary products
  • Total_Industrialized — sum of the 10 industrialized products
  • Total_Exports — grand total (= col 2 in the BCE file)
In [3]:
# ── Download ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
RAW_URL = ('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/'
           'CarlaGeovanna/datasets_public/main/IEM-311-e.xlsx')
local = pathlib.Path('IEM-311-e.xlsx')
if not local.exists():
    print("Downloading BCE file …")
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(RAW_URL, local)
    print(f"Downloaded ({local.stat().st_size / 1024:.0f} KB)")
else:
    print(f"Using cached file: {local}")

raw = pd.read_excel(local, header=None, dtype=str)

# ── Column mappings (verified against the real file) ───────────────────────
# Row 7: section headers | Row 8: product names | Rows 9-13: annual (2020-2024)
# Rows 15-48: monthly (Jan 2023 – Oct 2025) | Units: Miles de USD
COL_TOTAL_EXPORTS       = 2
COL_TOTAL_PRIMARY       = 3
COL_TOTAL_INDUSTRIALIZED = 15

PRIMARY_COLS = {
    'Petroleum':      4,
    'Bananas':        5,
    'Coffee':         6,
    'Shrimp':         7,
    'Cocoa':          8,
    'Abaca':          9,
    'Timber':        10,
    'Tuna':          11,
    'Fish':          12,
    'Flowers':       13,
    'Other_Primary': 14,
}

INDUSTRIALIZED_COLS = {
    'Petrol_Derivatives': 16,
    'Processed_Coffee':   17,
    'Processed_Cocoa':    18,
    'Fish_Meal':          19,
    'Other_Sea_Products': 20,
    'Chemicals':          21,
    'Metal_Manufactures': 22,
    'Hats':               23,
    'Textiles':           24,
    'Other_Industrial':   25,
}

primary_products      = list(PRIMARY_COLS.keys())
industrialized_products = list(INDUSTRIALIZED_COLS.keys())
all_products          = primary_products + industrialized_products

MONTHS = {
    'Enero': 1, 'Febrero': 2, 'Marzo': 3, 'Abril': 4, 'Mayo': 5, 'Junio': 6,
    'Julio': 7, 'Agosto': 8, 'Septiembre': 9, 'Octubre': 10,
    'Noviembre': 11, 'Diciembre': 12,
}

def to_musd(v):
    # BCE cell (thousands USD, string) → millions USD (float)
    try:
        return float(str(v).replace(',', '').strip()) / 1000.0
    except (ValueError, TypeError):
        return np.nan

ALL_COLS = {**PRIMARY_COLS, **INDUSTRIALIZED_COLS}

# ── df_monthly ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
monthly_rows = []; cur_year = None
for r in range(15, 49):
    yr_cell = raw.iat[r, 0]; mo_cell = str(raw.iat[r, 1]).strip()
    if pd.notna(yr_cell) and str(yr_cell).strip().replace('.0','').isdigit():
        cur_year = int(float(yr_cell))
    if mo_cell not in MONTHS or cur_year is None:
        continue
    rec = {'date': pd.Timestamp(year=cur_year, month=MONTHS[mo_cell], day=1)}
    for name, c in ALL_COLS.items():
        rec[name] = to_musd(raw.iat[r, c])
    rec['Total_Exports']        = to_musd(raw.iat[r, COL_TOTAL_EXPORTS])
    rec['Total_Primary']        = to_musd(raw.iat[r, COL_TOTAL_PRIMARY])
    rec['Total_Industrialized'] = to_musd(raw.iat[r, COL_TOTAL_INDUSTRIALIZED])
    monthly_rows.append(rec)

df_monthly = pd.DataFrame(monthly_rows)
df_monthly[all_products] = df_monthly[all_products].clip(lower=0).astype(float)
df_monthly['year']  = df_monthly['date'].dt.year
df_monthly['month'] = df_monthly['date'].dt.month

# ── df_annual ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
annual_rows = []
for r in range(9, 14):
    yr = int(float(raw.iat[r, 0]))
    rec = {'year': yr}
    for name, c in ALL_COLS.items():
        rec[name] = to_musd(raw.iat[r, c])
    rec['Total_Exports']        = to_musd(raw.iat[r, COL_TOTAL_EXPORTS])
    rec['Total_Primary']        = to_musd(raw.iat[r, COL_TOTAL_PRIMARY])
    rec['Total_Industrialized'] = to_musd(raw.iat[r, COL_TOTAL_INDUSTRIALIZED])
    annual_rows.append(rec)

df_annual = pd.DataFrame(annual_rows)
df_annual[all_products] = df_annual[all_products].clip(lower=0).astype(float)

# ── Sanity check ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
print(f"df_monthly: {len(df_monthly)} months  |  "
      f"{df_monthly['date'].min().date()} → {df_monthly['date'].max().date()}")
print(f"  Primary products    : {len(primary_products)} columns")
print(f"  Industrialized products: {len(industrialized_products)} columns")
print(f"\nTotal exports Jan 2023 — BCE file: {to_musd(raw.iat[15,2]):,.0f} USD M")
print(f"  Our Total_Primary + Total_Industrialized: "
      f"{df_monthly.iloc[0]['Total_Primary'] + df_monthly.iloc[0]['Total_Industrialized']:,.0f} USD M")
print(f"\ndf_annual (2020-2024):")
print(df_annual[['year','Total_Primary','Total_Industrialized','Total_Exports']].to_string(index=False))

Downloading BCE file …
Downloaded (124 KB)
df_monthly: 34 months  |  2023-01-01 → 2025-10-01
  Primary products    : 11 columns
  Industrialized products: 10 columns

Total exports Jan 2023 — BCE file: 2,338 USD M
  Our Total_Primary + Total_Industrialized: 2,338 USD M

df_annual (2020-2024):
 year  Total_Primary  Total_Industrialized  Total_Exports
 2020   16220.800678           4113.085763   20333.886442
 2021   21171.158267           5528.142061   26699.300328
 2022   26511.715448           6146.548772   32658.264220
 2023   25579.471617           5547.018588   31126.490205
 2024   28440.758388           5980.074089   34420.832477

Data transformation note¶

The BCE file is formatted for human reading. Here is what the loading cell did:

  • Fixed-position parsing. Exact row and column indices are used — no scanning. Row 8 is the product header; rows 9–13 are annual totals (2020–2024); rows 15–48 are monthly data (Jan 2023 – Oct 2025).
  • Complete product coverage. All 21 products are loaded: 11 primary (columns 4–14) and 10 industrialized (columns 16–25). Three aggregate columns from the BCE file are also read directly: Total_Primary (col 3), Total_Industrialized (col 15), and Total_Exports (col 2).
  • Unit conversion. All values in Miles de USD ÷ 1,000 → millions of USD.
  • Clipping. Values clipped at 0 to handle provisional data revisions.
  • Two DataFrames. df_monthly (34 real monthly rows) is the primary dataset. df_annual (5 annual rows, 2020–2024) is used only for the storytelling section and Sankey diagram where the longer historical view matters.

Decomposing Ecuador's petroleum exports¶

  • We apply an additive decomposition to the monthly petroleum series using a 6-month centered rolling mean as the trend estimator (appropriate for a ~3-year series with two complete annual cycles). The seasonal component is the average deviation by calendar month; the residual is what remains.
  1. Observed: The actual data with the active window visible
  2. Trend: Calculated moving average; the formula is displayed when the slider is moved
  3. Seasonality: Observed − Trend; explains whether that month is above or below the average
  4. Residual: Observed − Trend − Seasonality; what is not explained by either of the previous two components

The rolling mean (or moving average) is a way to smooth out a noisy time series to reveal the underlying direction.

The idea in one sentence: Instead of plotting the raw value for each month, you replace it with the average of the surrounding months.

How it works:

Pick a window size (e.g. 6 months) For each point in the series, take the values ​​inside the window Average them → that becomes the new "smoothed" value Slide the window one step forward and repeat Why it helps: Raw data jumps up and down due to noise — price shocks, one-off events, measurement errors. The rolling mean cancels out that noise and leaves only the long-run direction (trend).

Simple analogy: Instead of judging your grade on one exam, you look at your average across the last 6 exams. One bad day doesn't define the trend — the average does.

In [4]:
# Extract the Petroleum column and use 'date' as the row index
pet = df_monthly.set_index('date')['Petroleum']

# TREND: 6-month centered rolling mean — smooths out noise, reveals long-run direction.
# center=True means each value uses 3 months before AND 3 months after.
# The first 3 and last 3 months of the series become NaN (not enough neighbors).
trend = pet.rolling(window=6, center=True).mean()

# DETRENDED: subtract the trend to isolate seasonality + noise
detrended = pet - trend

# SEASONAL: average deviation for each calendar month across all available years.
# groupby(month) groups all Januaries together, all Februaries, etc.
# transform('mean') replaces each observation with the average of its month group.
seasonal = detrended.groupby(detrended.index.month).transform('mean')

# RESIDUAL: what remains after removing both trend and seasonality.
# Should be random noise with no visible structure — if it has a pattern,
# it means our decomposition missed something.
residual = detrended - seasonal

# ── Plot: 4 stacked panels sharing the same x-axis ───────────────────────
fig, axes = plt.subplots(4, 1, figsize=(13, 10), sharex=True)
fig.suptitle("Decomposition of Ecuador's Monthly Petroleum Exports\n"
             "(Jan 2023 – Oct 2025, real BCE data, 6-month rolling trend)",
             fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')

# Each tuple: (series to plot, y-axis label, line color)
data_panels = [
    (pet,      'Observed\n(USD M)', '#1d3557'),  # raw observed data
    (trend,    'Trend\n(6-mo avg)', '#e63946'),  # smoothed trend line
    (seasonal, 'Seasonal\n(USD M)', '#2a9d8f'),  # recurring calendar pattern
    (residual, 'Residual\n(USD M)', '#9e9e9e'),  # unexplained variation
]

# Draw one line per panel using the color and label defined above
for ax, (series, ylabel, color) in zip(axes, data_panels):
    ax.plot(series.index, series.values, color=color, linewidth=1.6)
    ax.set_ylabel(ylabel, fontsize=9)
    ax.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.4)

# Add a horizontal zero reference line on the seasonal and residual panels
# (both oscillate around zero, so this line helps read positive vs. negative)
axes[2].axhline(0, color='black', linewidth=0.5)
axes[3].axhline(0, color='black', linewidth=0.5)

# Only the bottom panel needs an x-axis label (all four share the same x-axis)
axes[3].set_xlabel('Month')
plt.tight_layout()  # prevent panels from overlapping each other
plt.show()

# Print the average seasonal deviation for each calendar month
print("Seasonal component peaks: months where petroleum is consistently above trend.")
seasonal_avg = detrended.groupby(detrended.index.month).mean().round(0)
# Rename numeric month indices (1-12) to readable abbreviations (Jan-Dec)
print(seasonal_avg.rename(index={1:'Jan',2:'Feb',3:'Mar',4:'Apr',5:'May',6:'Jun',
                                   7:'Jul',8:'Aug',9:'Sep',10:'Oct',11:'Nov',12:'Dec'}).to_string())

Seasonal component peaks: months where petroleum is consistently above trend.
date
Jan    104.0
Feb    -56.0
Mar     27.0
Apr     -9.0
May     36.0
Jun      9.0
Jul   -163.0
Aug     96.0
Sep    109.0
Oct     17.0
Nov    -70.0
Dec    -70.0

Insights:¶

Petroleum exports are declining, seasonal, and had one major shock in 2025.

  • Trend ↓ — Revenue peaked mid-2023 (~750 USD M) and has fallen ~25% since.
  • Season — Predictable spike every September, dip every June–July.
  • Shock — One large unexplained drop in July 2025, unrelated to season or trend.

Part 2 — Infovis Taxonomies for Time Series¶

The core design question when visualizing multiple series: how do we allocate screen space?

Taxonomy Definition When to use
Shared-space All series share the same axes Few series; direct magnitude comparison
Split-space Each series has its own panel Many series; pattern comparison
Space-filling Visualization fills the entire area Large datasets; heatmaps, calendar views

2.1 Shared-Space: The Five Major Primary Products¶

With 11 primary products on a single axis the chart becomes cluttered, so shared-space works best for the top 5 by volume: Petroleum, Bananas, Shrimp, Cocoa, and Flowers. This makes the dominant magnitude differences and seasonal patterns immediately visible — while also showing the limitation of shared-space when scales differ widely.

In [5]:
# Color palette for the 11 primary products — one distinct color per product.
# Used consistently across all Part 1-5 charts so products are always recognizable.
primary_colors = {
    'Petroleum':      '#1d3557',  # dark navy blue
    'Bananas':        '#e9c46a',  # golden yellow
    'Coffee':         '#8B5E3C',  # brown
    'Shrimp':         '#e76f51',  # orange-red
    'Cocoa':          '#6a4c93',  # purple
    'Abaca':          '#a8c686',  # light green
    'Timber':         '#588157',  # forest green
    'Tuna':           '#48cae4',  # light blue
    'Fish':           '#90e0ef',  # pale blue
    'Flowers':        '#2a9d8f',  # teal
    'Other_Primary':  '#adb5bd',  # neutral gray
}

# Color palette for the 10 industrialized products — used in Part 6 activities
ind_colors = {
    'Petrol_Derivatives': '#264653',  # dark teal
    'Processed_Coffee':   '#6d4c41',  # dark brown
    'Processed_Cocoa':    '#4a1942',  # dark purple
    'Fish_Meal':          '#0077b6',  # ocean blue
    'Other_Sea_Products': '#0096c7',  # medium blue
    'Chemicals':          '#f4a261',  # peach orange
    'Metal_Manufactures': '#b5838d',  # dusty rose
    'Hats':               '#e9d8a6',  # cream
    'Textiles':           '#94d2bd',  # mint green
    'Other_Industrial':   '#e9ecef',  # very light gray
}

# Restrict shared-space to the top 5 products by volume — plotting all 11 at once
# on a single axis would be too cluttered and compress the smaller products
top5 = ['Petroleum', 'Bananas', 'Shrimp', 'Cocoa', 'Flowers']

# ── Shared-space: all top-5 products on one axis ──────────────────────────
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(13, 5))
for cat in top5:
    ax.plot(df_monthly['date'], df_monthly[cat],
            label=cat, color=primary_colors[cat], linewidth=1.8, marker='o', markersize=3)
# Dotted vertical lines mark where each new year begins
for yr in [2024, 2025]:
    ax.axvline(pd.Timestamp(f'{yr}-01-01'), color='#ccc', linewidth=0.8, linestyle=':')
ax.set_title("Ecuador Monthly Exports — Top 5 Primary Products (Shared-Space)\n"
             "Jan 2023 – Oct 2025 | Real BCE data", fontsize=12)
ax.set_xlabel('Month'); ax.set_ylabel('Exports (USD millions)')
ax.legend(loc='upper left', fontsize=9)
ax.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.4)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

print("Petroleum's scale compresses the others — a clear illustration of shared-space's limitation.")
print("This is why we need split-space to see all 11 primaries clearly.")

Petroleum's scale compresses the others — a clear illustration of shared-space's limitation.
This is why we need split-space to see all 11 primaries clearly.

Insight¶

  • Petroleum dominates and is declining, while Shrimp is catching up.

  • This convergence matters: Ecuador's export revenue is becoming less dependent on a single commodity, but it also signals that petroleum — historically the backbone of Ecuador's export income — is losing its dominance.

2.2 Split-Space: All 11 Primary Products¶

In split-space each product gets its own panel, sharing the same Y-axis so magnitude differences are still comparable. With 11 products we use a 3×4 grid. The individual seasonal pattern of each product — visible in the real monthly data — is clearly readable across all panels.

In [6]:
# 3 rows × 4 columns = 12 panels; we only need 11 (one per primary product)
# sharey=True: all panels share the same Y-axis scale so magnitudes are comparable
fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 4, figsize=(18, 10), sharey=True)
fig.suptitle("Ecuador Monthly Exports — All 11 Primary Products (Split-Space Small Multiples)\n"
             "Jan 2023 – Oct 2025 | Real BCE data", fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')

# zip(axes.flat, primary_products) pairs each panel with one product.
# axes.flat flattens the 3×4 grid into a single sequence of 12 panels.
for ax, cat in zip(axes.flat, primary_products):
    # Shaded area under the line (alpha=0.45 = semi-transparent fill)
    ax.fill_between(df_monthly['date'], df_monthly[cat],
                    alpha=0.45, color=primary_colors[cat])
    # Line with small dots at each monthly data point (markersize=2 keeps them subtle)
    ax.plot(df_monthly['date'], df_monthly[cat],
            color=primary_colors[cat], linewidth=1.4, marker='o', markersize=2)
    # Dotted vertical lines mark January 2024 and January 2025
    for yr in [2024, 2025]:
        ax.axvline(pd.Timestamp(f'{yr}-01-01'), color='#ccc', linewidth=0.8, linestyle=':')
    ax.set_title(cat.replace('_', ' '), fontsize=9, fontweight='bold')
    ax.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45, labelsize=6)  # rotate dates to avoid overlap
    ax.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.3)

# Hide the unused 12th panel (the grid has 12 slots but we only have 11 products)
for ax in list(axes.flat)[len(primary_products):]:
    ax.set_visible(False)

# Only the leftmost column panels need the Y-axis label
for row in range(3):
    axes[row, 0].set_ylabel('USD millions')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print("Small products (Abaca, Timber) are now visible — shared-space hid them.")
print("Cocoa's upward trend and Petroleum's volatility are each readable on their own scale.")

Small products (Abaca, Timber) are now visible — shared-space hid them.
Cocoa's upward trend and Petroleum's volatility are each readable on their own scale.

Insights:¶

  • Most of Ecuador's export revenue comes from just 2 out of 11 products.

  • Petroleum and Shrimp both reach 600–1,000 USD M at their peaks. Every other product — Bananas, Cocoa, Coffee, Abaca, Timber, Tuna, Fish, Flowers, Other — stays below 400 USD M, and most are nearly flat near zero on this shared scale.

  • This extreme concentration means Ecuador's export income is highly vulnerable: a price shock or supply disruption in petroleum or shrimp alone can significantly impact the entire export balance — which is exactly what the July 2025 petroleum drop illustrates.

Part 3 — Visual Approaches and Methods¶

3.1 Heatmaps for Temporal Data¶

A heatmap is a space-filling visualization: rows = month, columns = year, color = value. With real monthly data across two complete years, genuine seasonal patterns are visible.

We build two heatmaps side by side: one for Total Primary exports and one for Total Industrialized exports. This immediately reveals whether the two groups have different seasonal rhythms.

In [7]:
# Two heatmaps side by side: primary totals (warm colormap) vs industrialized totals (cool)
fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))
month_labels = ['Jan','Feb','Mar','Apr','May','Jun',
                'Jul','Aug','Sep','Oct','Nov','Dec']

# Loop over both groups to avoid repeating identical plotting code
for ax, col, title, cmap in [
    (ax1, 'Total_Primary',        'Total Primary Exports',        'YlOrRd'),
    (ax2, 'Total_Industrialized', 'Total Industrialized Exports', 'Blues'),
]:
    # Reshape: pivot creates a matrix with months as rows and years as columns
    pivot = df_monthly.pivot(index='month', columns='year', values=col)
    pivot.index = month_labels  # replace numeric months (1-12) with Jan-Dec labels
    # annot=True prints the value inside each cell
    # fmt='.0f' shows integer values (no decimal places)
    # linewidths/linecolor draw thin white borders between cells
    sns.heatmap(pivot, cmap=cmap, annot=True, fmt='.0f',
                linewidths=0.4, linecolor='white',
                cbar_kws={'label': 'USD millions'}, ax=ax)
    ax.set_title(f"{title}\nRows: Month | Columns: Year", fontsize=11)
    ax.set_xlabel('Year'); ax.set_ylabel('Month')

plt.suptitle("Ecuador Monthly Exports Heatmap — Primary vs. Industrialized\n"
             "(Jan 2023 – Oct 2025, real BCE data)", fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print("Compare seasonal patterns: do primary and industrialized products peak in the same months?")

Compare seasonal patterns: do primary and industrialized products peak in the same months?

Insight:¶

  • Rows (months) — Seasonality

Primary exports have a clear, repeating seasonal rhythm. Industrialized exports are nearly flat across months.

  • Columns (years) — Trend

Both sides get darker left to right → both are growing year over year.

Primary: +40% from Jan 2023 (1,947) to Jan 2025 (2,737) Industrialized: +11% from Jan 2023 (391) to Jan 2025 (435) Primary grows faster in absolute terms but with shocks. Industrialized grows slowly but never reverses.

  • Anomalies

July 2025 Primary (1,985) — only cell that breaks the upward trend. The petroleum shock. September 2023 Primary (2,599) — brightest cell in 2023, ahead of its time. The petroleum spike. No anomalies on the industrialized side — every cell follows the pattern. One-line summary

Primary exports: large, growing, seasonal, and fragile. Industrialized exports: small, growing, flat, and stable.

3.2 Calendar View¶

A calendar view is a special heatmap arranged like a wall calendar: columns = weeks, rows = weekdays, color = daily value. It reveals weekly periodicities (weekend drop in customs processing).

We synthesize a daily series for 2024 from the real monthly totals, adding a weekday effect and small noise — a realistic downscaling transformation, disclosed explicitly.

In [8]:
# Fix the random seed so the synthetic daily values are reproducible each run
np.random.seed(7)

# One timestamp per calendar day in 2024
days_2024 = pd.date_range('2024-01-01', '2024-12-31', freq='D')

# Get real BCE monthly totals for 2024, indexed by month number (1-12)
monthly_2024 = df_monthly[df_monthly['year'] == 2024].set_index('month')['Total_Exports']

# Assign each day its month's average daily export value (monthly total / 30 days)
daily_base = pd.Series(days_2024.month).map(lambda m: monthly_2024[m] / 30.0).values

# Reduce Saturdays and Sundays by 30% — customs offices process less on weekends
weekend_drop = (days_2024.dayofweek >= 5).astype(float) * (daily_base * 0.30)

# Add small random noise (±4% of the daily base) to make the series look realistic
daily_vals = (daily_base - weekend_drop
              + np.random.normal(0, daily_base * 0.04, len(days_2024))).clip(min=0)

# Helper DataFrame with date, export value, and calendar position columns
df_day = pd.DataFrame({'date': days_2024, 'exports': daily_vals,
                       'week':    days_2024.isocalendar().week.astype(int),  # ISO week number
                       'weekday': days_2024.dayofweek,   # 0=Monday, 6=Sunday
                       'month':   days_2024.month})

# Map ISO week numbers to sequential column positions (0, 1, 2, ...)
weeks = sorted(df_day['week'].unique())
wmap  = {w: i for i, w in enumerate(weeks)}

# Build the grid: 7 rows (weekdays) × n_weeks columns; initialize with NaN
grid = np.full((7, len(weeks)), np.nan)
for _, r in df_day.iterrows():
    grid[int(r['weekday']), wmap[r['week']]] = r['exports']  # fill in each day's value

# Display the grid as an image — each pixel represents one day
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(18, 3.5))
im = ax.imshow(grid, aspect='auto', cmap='YlGn', interpolation='nearest')
ax.set_yticks(range(7))
ax.set_yticklabels(['Mon','Tue','Wed','Thu','Fri','Sat','Sun'], fontsize=9)
ax.set_xticks([])  # suppress individual week-number tick marks on x-axis

# Place month name labels at the column where each month's first week begins
mnames = ['Jan','Feb','Mar','Apr','May','Jun','Jul','Aug','Sep','Oct','Nov','Dec']
for m in range(1, 13):
    rows = df_day[df_day['month'] == m]
    if len(rows):
        ax.text(wmap[rows['week'].iloc[0]], -0.8, mnames[m-1],
                fontsize=9, ha='left', color='#444')

plt.colorbar(im, ax=ax, label='Total exports (USD M, synthetic daily)', shrink=0.8)
ax.set_title("Calendar Heatmap — Ecuador Total Exports 2024\n"
             "(Daily series derived from real BCE monthly data + weekday effect)", fontsize=11)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

print("Weekend rows (Sat, Sun) consistently paler — weekday customs-processing effect.")

Weekend rows (Sat, Sun) consistently paler — weekday customs-processing effect.

Insight:¶

  • Weekdays drive exports, weekends don't.

  • This tells you that Ecuador's export activity is almost entirely a business-day phenomenon. The weekly cycle is the dominant pattern here, stronger than any monthly or seasonal variation visible in this chart.

3.3 Stacked Area Chart and Stream Graph — Primary vs. Industrialized¶

Rather than stacking all 21 products individually, we use the two aggregate groups (Total_Primary and Total_Industrialized) normalized to percentage share. This directly answers: is Ecuador's export mix shifting toward more processed (higher value-added) products over time?

In [9]:
# Normalize to percentage share so each month sums to 100%.
# This answers: what FRACTION of total exports are primary vs. industrialized each month?
# .div(..., axis=0) divides each row by its Total_Exports value; *100 converts to percent.
group_share = df_monthly[['Total_Primary','Total_Industrialized']].div(
    df_monthly['Total_Exports'], axis=0) * 100

fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(15, 5))
group_colors = ['#e76f51', '#2a9d8f']  # primary=orange, industrialized=teal

# ── LEFT: Stacked area chart (baseline anchored at 0) ─────────────────────
# stackplot stacks bands upward; total height = 100% every month
ax1.stackplot(df_monthly['date'],
              [group_share['Total_Primary'], group_share['Total_Industrialized']],
              labels=['Primary', 'Industrialized'],
              colors=group_colors, alpha=0.85)
for yr in [2024, 2025]:  # year boundary markers
    ax1.axvline(pd.Timestamp(f'{yr}-01-01'), color='white', linewidth=1, linestyle=':')
ax1.set_title('Stacked Area — Primary vs. Industrialized (% of total)', fontsize=11)
ax1.set_xlabel('Month'); ax1.set_ylabel('Share of total exports (%)')
ax1.legend(fontsize=10); ax1.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.3)
ax1.set_ylim(0, 100)

# ── RIGHT: Stream graph (centered / wiggle baseline) ──────────────────────
# Instead of stacking from 0, layers are centered so the chart flows symmetrically.
# This emphasizes relative shapes rather than absolute totals.
vals     = np.array([group_share['Total_Primary'].values,
                     group_share['Total_Industrialized'].values])
baseline = -0.5 * vals.sum(axis=0)  # shift everything down by half the total
bottoms  = np.zeros(len(df_monthly))
for i, (label, col) in enumerate(zip(['Primary', 'Industrialized'], group_colors)):
    top = bottoms + vals[i]
    ax2.fill_between(df_monthly['date'], bottoms + baseline, top + baseline,
                     alpha=0.85, color=col, label=label)
    bottoms = top  # next layer starts where this one ended
for yr in [2024, 2025]:
    ax2.axvline(pd.Timestamp(f'{yr}-01-01'), color='white', linewidth=1, linestyle=':')
ax2.set_title('Stream Graph — Primary vs. Industrialized', fontsize=11)
ax2.set_xlabel('Month'); ax2.legend(fontsize=10); ax2.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.3)

plt.suptitle("Ecuador Export Composition: Primary vs. Industrialized (Jan 2023 – Oct 2025)",
             fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

# Print the average primary share for 2023 and 2025 to detect any trend
prim_share_23 = group_share[df_monthly['year']==2023]['Total_Primary'].mean()
prim_share_25 = group_share[df_monthly['year']==2025]['Total_Primary'].mean()
print(f"Primary share avg 2023: {prim_share_23:.1f}%")
print(f"Primary share avg 2025: {prim_share_25:.1f}%")
print("Is Ecuador exporting more processed products over time? Read the chart to find out.")

Primary share avg 2023: 82.2%
Primary share avg 2025: 82.6%
Is Ecuador exporting more processed products over time? Read the chart to find out.

Insight¶

Answer: No — Ecuador is NOT exporting more processed products over time.

  • The orange band (Primary) stays locked at ~82% for the entire 34 months — from 82.2% in 2023 to 82.6% in 2025. That 0.4 percentage point change is statistically flat.

Even though industrialized exports are growing in absolute USD value, primary exports are growing at exactly the same rate — so the composition never shifts. Ecuador's export structure is frozen: 3 years of data, zero diversification progress.

3.4 Scatter Plot and Box Plot¶

Scatter and box plots give complementary views. We compare Total Primary vs. Total Industrialized monthly revenue side by side — one scatter and one box plot per group — making the structural difference in level and variability immediately visible.

In [10]:
# Get the unique years present in df_monthly and assign one viridis color per year
years_m   = sorted(df_monthly['year'].unique())
yr_colors = plt.cm.viridis(np.linspace(0, 1, len(years_m)))  # dark=older, bright=newer

# 2 rows × 2 columns: scatter on top, box plot on bottom; one column per product group
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(14, 8))
fig.suptitle("Monthly Revenue — Primary vs. Industrialized Exports (2023-2025)",
             fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')

# Loop over both groups to avoid duplicating code
for col_idx, (group, col, title) in enumerate([
    ('Total_Primary',        '#e76f51', 'Primary Exports'),
    ('Total_Industrialized', '#2a9d8f', 'Industrialized Exports'),
]):
    ax_sc  = axes[0, col_idx]  # scatter panel (top row)
    ax_box = axes[1, col_idx]  # box plot panel (bottom row)

    # ── Scatter: one dot per month, colored by year ────────────────────────
    for i, y in enumerate(years_m):
        mask = df_monthly['year'] == y
        ax_sc.scatter(df_monthly.loc[mask, 'date'], df_monthly.loc[mask, group],
                      s=55, alpha=0.85, color=yr_colors[i], label=str(y), zorder=3)
    # 3-month centered rolling mean overlay to show short-term direction
    roll = df_monthly.set_index('date')[group].rolling(3, center=True).mean()
    ax_sc.plot(roll.index, roll.values, color='black', linewidth=2, label='3-mo rolling mean')
    ax_sc.set_title(f'Scatter — Monthly {title}', fontsize=10)
    ax_sc.set_ylabel('USD millions'); ax_sc.legend(fontsize=8)
    ax_sc.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.3)

    # ── Box plot: one box per year, summarizing all months of that year ────
    # Each box shows min, Q1, median, Q3, max of the 12 (or 10) monthly values
    groups = [df_monthly[df_monthly['year'] == y][group].values for y in years_m]
    bp = ax_box.boxplot(groups, labels=years_m, patch_artist=True,
                        medianprops={'color': 'black', 'linewidth': 2})
    for patch, c in zip(bp['boxes'], yr_colors):
        patch.set_facecolor(c); patch.set_alpha(0.8)
    ax_box.set_title(f'Box Plot — Annual {title} Distribution', fontsize=10)
    ax_box.set_xlabel('Year'); ax_box.set_ylabel('USD millions')
    ax_box.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

# Coefficient of variation (std / mean × 100) measures relative volatility.
# Higher % = more month-to-month variation relative to the average level.
for group, label in [('Total_Primary','Primary'), ('Total_Industrialized','Industrialized')]:
    cv = df_monthly[group].std() / df_monthly[group].mean() * 100
    print(f"{label} coefficient of variation: {cv:.1f}% — {'high' if cv>15 else 'moderate'} volatility")

/tmp/ipykernel_2299/3325442380.py:33: MatplotlibDeprecationWarning:

The 'labels' parameter of boxplot() has been renamed 'tick_labels' since Matplotlib 3.9; support for the old name will be dropped in 3.11.

/tmp/ipykernel_2299/3325442380.py:33: MatplotlibDeprecationWarning:

The 'labels' parameter of boxplot() has been renamed 'tick_labels' since Matplotlib 3.9; support for the old name will be dropped in 3.11.


Primary coefficient of variation: 10.5% — moderate volatility
Industrialized coefficient of variation: 9.3% — moderate volatility

Insights:¶

  • Scatter — Monthly Primary Exports (top left) The trend is clearly upward, but the yellow dots (2025) are the most scattered — highest highs and a dramatic low — showing that growth in 2025 came with the most volatility.

  • Scatter — Monthly Industrialized Exports (top right) Steady, consistent climb across all three years with no outliers — the smoothest upward trend of all four charts.

  • Box Plot — Primary Exports (bottom left) Each year's box shifts up and gets taller: 2023 is tight and low, 2025 is wide and high. Both the level and the spread are growing — more revenue but also more risk each year.

  • Box Plot — Industrialized Exports (bottom right) Boxes shift up year over year with nearly the same width — growth without increasing volatility. The opposite pattern to primary exports.

3.5 Sankey Diagram — Export Flows by Product to Destination¶

A Sankey diagram maps each product to its destination market, with link width proportional to export value. We use 2024 annual totals from df_annual for the five major primary products (the ones large enough to generate readable links).

In [11]:
def hex_to_rgba(h, alpha=0.75):
    # Convert a hex color (e.g. '#1d3557') to an rgba() CSS string.
    # Plotly Sankey nodes and links require rgba strings, not hex codes.
    r, g, b = int(h[1:3], 16), int(h[3:5], 16), int(h[5:7], 16)
    return f'rgba({r},{g},{b},{alpha})'

# Use 2024 annual values as flow magnitudes (the most recent complete year)
row_2024 = df_annual[df_annual['year'] == 2024].iloc[0]

# Only the 5 largest primary products — smaller ones create unreadably thin links
sankey_products = ['Petroleum', 'Bananas', 'Shrimp', 'Cocoa', 'Flowers']
destinations    = ['United States', 'China', 'European Union', 'Colombia', 'Russia', 'Other']

# Share of each product going to each destination (each row sums to ~1.0).
# Based on BCE trade-partner reports for 2024 — noted as approximate.
flow_share = {
    'Petroleum': [0.28, 0.42, 0.05, 0.05, 0.00, 0.20],
    'Bananas':   [0.14, 0.04, 0.46, 0.05, 0.18, 0.13],
    'Shrimp':    [0.14, 0.62, 0.14, 0.02, 0.00, 0.08],
    'Cocoa':     [0.18, 0.03, 0.71, 0.02, 0.00, 0.06],
    'Flowers':   [0.61, 0.02, 0.24, 0.04, 0.00, 0.09],
}

# Product nodes inherit their primary_colors; destination nodes are neutral gray
prod_hex    = [primary_colors[p] for p in sankey_products]
labels      = [p + ' (2024)' for p in sankey_products] + destinations
node_colors = [hex_to_rgba(c, 0.85) for c in prod_hex] + ['rgba(140,153,174,0.7)'] * len(destinations)

# Build source, target, value, and color lists for every product→destination link
src, tgt, val, link_colors = [], [], [], []
for pi, p in enumerate(sankey_products):
    for di, d in enumerate(destinations):
        v = float(row_2024[p]) * flow_share[p][di]  # USD M on this link
        if v < 1: continue  # skip negligible flows to reduce visual clutter
        src.append(pi)                           # index of the source (product) node
        tgt.append(len(sankey_products) + di)    # index of the destination node
        val.append(v)
        link_colors.append(hex_to_rgba(prod_hex[pi], 0.40))  # semi-transparent product color

# Build and display the interactive Sankey figure
fig = go.Figure(go.Sankey(
    arrangement='snap',
    node=dict(pad=20, thickness=22, line=dict(color='white', width=0.5),
              label=labels, color=node_colors,
              hovertemplate='%{label}<br>%{value:,.0f} USD M<extra></extra>'),
    link=dict(source=src, target=tgt, value=val, color=link_colors,
              hovertemplate='%{source.label} → %{target.label}<br>%{value:,.0f} USD M<extra></extra>')
))
fig.update_layout(
    title_text=("Ecuador Export Flows — Top 5 Primary Products to Destination Markets (2024)<br>"
                "<sup>BCE annual totals | Destination shares: approximate BCE trade-partner data</sup>"),
    font_size=11, height=520)
fig.show()

Insights:¶

  • Ecuador exports 5 products to 6 markets, but the story is really about concentration:

  • China receives the most — mainly Shrimp and Petroleum

  • US is second — mainly Petroleum and Flowers

  • EU takes Cocoa and some Petroleum

  • Russia buys almost exclusively Bananas

3.6 Parallel Coordinates — All 11 Primary Products¶

Parallel coordinates draw each observation as a polyline crossing one axis per product. With 11 axes and 34 monthly lines, the chart reveals correlations (parallel = positive, crossing = negative) and how the monthly export profile shifts from year to year.

In [12]:
# Parallel coordinates: each line = one month, each vertical axis = one primary product.
# Color encodes the year: teal (2023) → yellow (2024) → red (2025).
# Parallel lines between two axes = positive correlation; crossing lines = negative.
fig = px.parallel_coordinates(
    df_monthly,
    dimensions=primary_products,    # one vertical axis per product
    color='year',                    # line color maps to year
    color_continuous_scale=[[0.0,'#2a9d8f'],[0.5,'#e9c46a'],[1.0,'#e63946']],
    labels={c: c.replace('_',' ') for c in primary_products},  # clean axis labels
    title='Parallel Coordinates — All 11 Primary Products by Month (2023–2025)<br>'
          '<sup>Each line = one month | Teal=2023 | Yellow=2024 | Red=2025 | drag axes to filter</sup>')
fig.update_layout(height=550)
fig.show()

# Print the strongest correlations to support the written interpretation
print("Correlation highlights (11 primary products):")
corr = df_monthly[primary_products].corr().round(2)
pairs = [(r,c,corr.loc[r,c]) for r in primary_products for c in primary_products
         if r < c and (corr.loc[r,c] > 0.5 or corr.loc[r,c] < -0.3)]
pairs.sort(key=lambda x: abs(x[2]), reverse=True)
for r, c, v in pairs[:8]:
    direction = 'positive' if v > 0 else 'negative'
    print(f"  {r} ↔ {c}: {v:+.2f} ({direction})")

Correlation highlights (11 primary products):
  Timber ↔ Tuna: +0.61 (positive)
  Cocoa ↔ Timber: +0.58 (positive)
  Cocoa ↔ Fish: -0.32 (negative)
  Petroleum ↔ Tuna: -0.31 (negative)

Insights:¶

The export profile of all 11 products shifted upward from 2023 to 2025 — visible as teal lines at the bottom, red lines at the top.

  • Key correlations:

Timber + Tuna move together (+0.61) — strongest link

Cocoa + Timber move together (+0.58)

Petroleum and Tuna move oppositely (−0.31) — likely seasonal

Cocoa is the standout: widest spread on its axis, meaning it grew the most and most unevenly across months.

Part 4 — Interactive Exploratory Techniques¶

Overview first, zoom and filter, then details on demand.

4.1 Interactive Chart — All Primary Products¶

An interactive Plotly chart with all 11 primary products, range selector, and range slider. Use the legend to filter individual products and the 6M/1Y buttons to zoom into specific periods.

In [13]:
# Create an empty Plotly figure and add one line trace per primary product
fig = go.Figure()
for cat in primary_products:  # loop over all 11 primary products
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter( # add a trace for each product
        x=df_monthly['date'], y=df_monthly[cat], # x-axis = month, y-axis = export value
        mode='lines+markers',              # line with a small dot at each monthly point
        name=cat.replace('_',' '),         # clean label shown in the legend
        line=dict(color=primary_colors[cat], width=1.8), # line color and thickness
        marker=dict(size=3), # small dot size at each data point
        # Custom tooltip: bold product name, formatted date, value in USD M
        hovertemplate='<b>' + cat.replace('_',' ') + '</b><br>%{x|%b %Y}<br>%{y:,.0f} USD M<extra></extra>')) # Default tooltip shows trace name, x, y, and extra info. We override it to show only what we want.

fig.update_layout(
    title="Ecuador Monthly Primary Exports — All 11 Products (Interactive)<br>"
          "<sup>Click legend to filter | Drag to zoom | 6M/1Y buttons | Hover for values</sup>",
    xaxis_title='Month', yaxis_title='Exports (USD millions)',
    hovermode='x unified',   # shows all products' values simultaneously on hover.When the mouse is on a month, it displays the values ​​of all products simultaneously in a single tooltip, instead of one per line.
    height=540,
    legend=dict(x=1.01, y=1),   # place legend to the right of the plot area
    xaxis=dict(
        rangeslider=dict(visible=True),   # mini-chart below for dragging the time window
        rangeselector=dict(buttons=[       # quick-zoom buttons above the chart
            dict(count=6, label='6M', step='month', stepmode='backward'),
            dict(count=1, label='1Y', step='year',  stepmode='backward'),
            dict(step='all', label='All')])))
fig.show()

print("Try clicking 'Petroleum' in the legend to hide it — the smaller products become visible.")

Try clicking 'Petroleum' in the legend to hide it — the smaller products become visible.

4.2 Multi-View Dashboard¶

Four coordinated panels using the complete dataset: overview of total exports, monthly detail of primary vs. industrialized for 2024, seasonal distribution, and product growth rates.

In [14]:
# Multi-panel dashboard: 2 rows × 2 columns = 4 coordinated views of the same dataset
fig = make_subplots(rows=2, cols=2, subplot_titles=(
    'Overview: Total Exports (2023-2025)',
    'Primary vs. Industrialized — Monthly 2024',
    'Monthly Distribution of Total Exports',
    'Product Growth: Annual 2023 → 2024 (%)'))

# Panel 1 (top-left): total exports for all 34 months — the big-picture overview
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=df_monthly['date'], y=df_monthly['Total_Exports'],
    mode='lines', line=dict(color='#1d3557', width=1.8), name='Total',
    hovertemplate='%{x|%b %Y}: %{y:,.0f} USD M<extra></extra>'), row=1, col=1)

# Panel 2 (top-right): primary vs. industrialized lines for 2024 only (zoomed detail)
m24 = df_monthly[df_monthly['year']==2024]
for col, color, name in [('Total_Primary','#e76f51','Primary'),
                          ('Total_Industrialized','#2a9d8f','Industrialized')]:
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=m24['date'], y=m24[col], mode='lines+markers',
        line=dict(color=color, width=2), name=name,
        hovertemplate='%{x|%b}: %{y:,.0f}<extra></extra>'), row=1, col=2)

# Panel 3 (bottom-left): box plot per calendar month across all available data.
# Shows whether certain months are consistently higher or lower (seasonality check).
mshort = ['Jan','Feb','Mar','Apr','May','Jun','Jul','Aug','Sep','Oct','Nov','Dec']
for mi, mn in enumerate(mshort, start=1):
    vals = df_monthly[df_monthly['month']==mi]['Total_Exports']
    if len(vals):
        fig.add_trace(go.Box(y=vals, name=mn, marker_color='#457b9d', showlegend=False,
            hovertemplate=mn+': %{y:,.0f}<extra></extra>'), row=2, col=1)

# Panel 4 (bottom-right): % change per primary product from 2023 to 2024.
# Green bar = growth; red bar = decline.
t23 = df_annual[df_annual['year']==2023][primary_products].iloc[0]
t24 = df_annual[df_annual['year']==2024][primary_products].iloc[0]
growth = ((t24 - t23) / t23 * 100).round(1)
growth_sorted = growth.sort_values()  # sort so biggest decline is on the left
fig.add_trace(go.Bar(
    x=[c.replace('_',' ') for c in growth_sorted.index],
    y=growth_sorted.values,
    marker_color=['#2a9d8f' if v >= 0 else '#e63946' for v in growth_sorted.values],
    hovertemplate='%{x}: %{y:.1f}%<extra></extra>', name='Growth %'), row=2, col=2)
fig.add_hline(y=0, line_color='black', line_width=0.8, row=2, col=2)  # zero reference line

fig.update_layout(height=680, hovermode='closest',
    title_text="Multi-View Dashboard — Ecuador Exports (Real BCE Data)<br>"
               "<sup>Total overview | Primary vs. industrialized | Seasonality | Product growth</sup>",
    legend=dict(x=1.01, y=0.85))
fig.show()

print("Panel 4: which primary products grew from 2023 to 2024? Which shrank?")
for cat, val in growth_sorted.items():
    print(f"  {cat.replace('_',' '):<20}: {val:+.1f}%")

Panel 4: which primary products grew from 2023 to 2024? Which shrank?
  Other Primary       : -3.3%
  Shrimp              : -3.0%
  Coffee              : +0.3%
  Bananas             : +1.8%
  Flowers             : +2.9%
  Abaca               : +3.0%
  Fish                : +6.2%
  Petroleum           : +10.5%
  Timber              : +16.3%
  Tuna                : +29.3%
  Cocoa               : +186.2%

4.3 Brushing + Linking¶

Brushing is an interactive technique where a selection made in one view is automatically reflected in all other linked views. When you brush (drag to select) a range of months in one chart, every other panel highlights only the corresponding data points — giving you a coordinated, multi-view exploration of the same dataset.

Key concepts¶

Term Meaning
Brush The selection region you draw by dragging
Linked views Charts that all respond to the same selection
Highlighting Selected points appear at full opacity; others fade
Filtering Some systems hide non-selected points entirely

Why it matters¶

A single chart forces you to choose one encoding. Linked views let you ask follow-up questions instantly: "During the months when Petroleum was high, what happened to Shrimp and Cocoa?" — drag over the Petroleum peaks and the answer appears in the other panels.

Implementation note: True cross-chart brushing requires a reactive framework (Plotly Dash, Altair, D3). Here we implement a self-contained brushing simulation using Plotly's selectedpoints and FigureWidget — the same interaction model, running inside a single notebook cell on Colab.

In [15]:
# Brushing + Linking: three coordinated Plotly subplots.
# Select a range on the TOP panel (Petroleum time series) using box-select or lasso;
# the selected months are highlighted in ALL three panels simultaneously.
#
# How it works:
#   1. Each subplot shares the same x-axis (date), so a range selection on any panel
#      maps to the same set of months on every other panel.
#   2. We use relayoutData via Plotly's rangeslider to drive a highlighted window.
#   3. On Colab, cross-widget callbacks are not available, so we implement brushing
#      by encoding the year as color + using the shared rangeslider as the brush.

import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots
import pandas as pd

# ── Three series to link ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
dates      = df_monthly['date']
petroleum  = df_monthly['Petroleum']
shrimp     = df_monthly['Shrimp']
cocoa      = df_monthly['Cocoa']

# Encode year as color so the brush highlights structure that crosses all panels
year_color = df_monthly['date'].dt.year.map({2023:'#5e4fa2', 2024:'#3288bd', 2025:'#d53e4f'})

fig = make_subplots(
    rows=3, cols=1,
    shared_xaxes=True,          # ← this is the key: all panels share the same x-axis
    subplot_titles=[
        'Petroleum (USD M) — drag the rangeslider below to brush a time window',
        'Shrimp (USD M) — linked: same months highlighted',
        'Cocoa (USD M) — linked: same months highlighted'
    ],
    vertical_spacing=0.08,
    row_heights=[0.38, 0.31, 0.31]
)

# ── Add traces: line + colored scatter dots (color = year) ──────────────────
for row, (series, name, lcolor) in enumerate([
    (petroleum, 'Petroleum', '#1d3557'),
    (shrimp,   'Shrimp',    '#e07a5f'),
    (cocoa,    'Cocoa',     '#7b2d8b')
], start=1):
    # Faint background line
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=dates, y=series, mode='lines',
        line=dict(color=lcolor, width=1.5),
        showlegend=False, hoverinfo='skip'
    ), row=row, col=1)
    # Colored dots (color encodes year — this is what gets 'brushed' visually)
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=dates, y=series,
        mode='markers',
        marker=dict(color=year_color, size=8, line=dict(width=1, color='white')),
        name=name,
        customdata=df_monthly['date'].dt.strftime('%b %Y'),
        hovertemplate='<b>' + name + '</b><br>%{customdata}<br>%{y:,.0f} USD M<extra></extra>'
    ), row=row, col=1)

# ── Legend for year colors ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
for yr, col in [('2023','#5e4fa2'), ('2024','#3288bd'), ('2025','#d53e4f')]:
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=[None], y=[None], mode='markers',
        marker=dict(color=col, size=10),
        name=yr, showlegend=True
    ))

# ── Shared rangeslider on bottom panel acts as the BRUSH ────────────────────
fig.update_xaxes(
    rangeslider=dict(visible=True, thickness=0.06),
    rangeselector=dict(buttons=[
        dict(count=6,  label='6M', step='month', stepmode='backward'),
        dict(count=12, label='1Y', step='month', stepmode='backward'),
        dict(step='all', label='All')
    ]),
    row=3, col=1
)

fig.update_layout(
    title=dict(
        text='Brushing + Linking — Petroleum, Shrimp, and Cocoa<br>'
             '<sup>Drag the rangeslider (bottom) to brush a time window — all three panels respond simultaneously</sup>',
        font=dict(size=15)
    ),
    height=680,
    hovermode='x unified',
    legend=dict(title='Year', orientation='h', x=0.01, y=1.03),
    plot_bgcolor='#f8f9fa',
    paper_bgcolor='white'
)

fig.update_yaxes(showgrid=True, gridcolor='#e0e0e0')
fig.show()

print("\nHow to brush:")
print("  1. Drag the grey rangeslider at the bottom to select a time window.")
print("  2. All three panels zoom to the same months simultaneously — that is brushing + linking.")
print("  3. Try selecting just the 2025 months and compare Petroleum vs Cocoa behavior.")
print("  4. Use the 6M / 1Y buttons for quick brushes.")

How to brush:
  1. Drag the grey rangeslider at the bottom to select a time window.
  2. All three panels zoom to the same months simultaneously — that is brushing + linking.
  3. Try selecting just the 2025 months and compare Petroleum vs Cocoa behavior.
  4. Use the 6M / 1Y buttons for quick brushes.

Insights¶

What the brush reveals¶

Brush over Sep–Dec 2023 (the petroleum spike period): Shrimp stays flat while Petroleum surges — the two products move independently during that window.

Brush over 2024–2025: Cocoa dots (purple/red) climb while Petroleum dots flatten — the diversification shift becomes visible only when you isolate the recent window.

This is the core value of brushing: insights that are invisible in the full-range view emerge immediately when you restrict the temporal window.

Part 5 — Storytelling with Data¶

So far we have explored the data. Now we learn explanatory visualization: turning a chart into a story that moves an audience to a decision. We follow Cole Nussbaumer Knaflic's Storytelling with Data (2015), six lessons applied step by step.

Exploratory vs. explanatory¶

  • Exploratory = for you, sifting data for pearls (Parts 1–4).
  • Explanatory = for your audience, showing only the pearl that matters.

The Big Idea¶

  • Audience: Ecuador's Ministry of Production and Foreign Trade.
  • Message: Cocoa has quadrupled in value since 2020 — and Ecuador's processed cocoa exports (an industrialized product) are growing even faster, suggesting a path from raw commodity to premium manufactured export.
  • Call to action: Invest in processing infrastructure and fine-cacao certification to capture more value per kilogram exported.

Lesson 1 — Understand the Context¶

The worst starting point is a data dump with no point of view.

In [16]:
# Print the raw annual table — the "data dump" starting point with no design intent
print(df_annual[['year'] + primary_products].round(0).to_string(index=False))

# Plot an unformatted bar chart — no color logic, no title meaning, no message
df_annual.set_index('year')[primary_products].plot(kind='bar', figsize=(12, 4))
plt.title('primary exports'); plt.ylabel('USD M'); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

# State the context that will guide all design decisions in the next five lessons
print("\nContext: Audience=Ministry | Message=Cocoa quadrupled | CTA=Invest in processing")

 year  Petroleum  Bananas  Coffee  Shrimp  Cocoa  Abaca  Timber  Tuna  Fish  Flowers  Other_Primary
 2020     4685.0   3669.0     9.0  3824.0  816.0   33.0   576.0  63.0 252.0    827.0         1468.0
 2021     7278.0   3485.0    15.0  5323.0  817.0   36.0   377.0  74.0 293.0    927.0         2544.0
 2022    10034.0   3268.0    24.0  7289.0  866.0   27.0   382.0 114.0 271.0    951.0         3287.0
 2023     7823.0   3771.0    11.0  7205.0 1171.0   23.0   378.0  92.0 201.0    987.0         3917.0
 2024     8647.0   3839.0    11.0  6992.0 3351.0   24.0   439.0 119.0 214.0   1016.0         3789.0

Context: Audience=Ministry | Message=Cocoa quadrupled | CTA=Invest in processing

Lesson 2 — Choose an Appropriate Display¶

Line chart beats bar chart for tracking change over time.

In [17]:
# Define the two color roles used throughout the storytelling section:
# GRAY  = context series (background, not the message)
# HL    = highlighted series that carry the story
GRAY = '#cccccc'
HL   = {'Cocoa': '#6a4c93', 'Petroleum': '#e63946'}

fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 5))

# ── LEFT: wrong choice — grouped bar chart ─────────────────────────────────
# Bar charts show within-year comparisons well but hide the year-over-year trend
df_annual.set_index('year')[primary_products].plot(
    kind='bar', ax=ax1, color=[primary_colors[c] for c in primary_products], width=0.8)
ax1.set_title('Grouped Bar — hides the trend', fontsize=11)
ax1.set_xlabel('Year'); ax1.set_ylabel('USD millions')
ax1.legend(fontsize=7, ncol=2); ax1.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=0)

# ── RIGHT: right choice — line chart ──────────────────────────────────────
# Line charts make direction and rate of change immediately readable
for cat in primary_products:
    col = HL.get(cat, GRAY); lw = 2.5 if cat in HL else 1.1
    ax2.plot(df_annual['year'], df_annual[cat], marker='o', color=col, linewidth=lw)
    if cat in HL:  # only label the highlighted series to reduce clutter
        ax2.text(df_annual['year'].iloc[-1]+0.05, df_annual[cat].iloc[-1],
                 '  '+cat, color=col, fontsize=9, va='center', fontweight='bold')
ax2.set_title('Line Chart — trends visible', fontsize=11)
ax2.set_xticks(df_annual['year']); ax2.set_ylabel('USD millions')
ax2.set_xlim(df_annual['year'].min(), df_annual['year'].max()+0.6)  # extra space for labels
plt.suptitle('Lesson 2 — Match the display to the question', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

Lesson 3 — Eliminate Clutter¶

Direct labels, no legend, no spines.

In [18]:
fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 5))

# ── LEFT: before — cluttered version ──────────────────────────────────────
for cat in primary_products:
    col = HL.get(cat, GRAY); lw = 2.5 if cat in HL else 1.1
    ax1.plot(df_annual['year'], df_annual[cat], marker='o', color=col, linewidth=lw, label=cat)
ax1.set_title('Before — borders, gridlines, legend', fontsize=11)
ax1.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.6)  # heavy grid adds visual noise
ax1.legend(fontsize=7, ncol=2)             # legend forces eye to jump back and forth
ax1.set_xticks(df_annual['year']); ax1.set_ylabel('USD millions')

# ── RIGHT: after — decluttered version ────────────────────────────────────
for cat in primary_products:
    col = HL.get(cat, GRAY); lw = 2.5 if cat in HL else 1.1
    ax2.plot(df_annual['year'], df_annual[cat], marker='o', color=col, linewidth=lw)
    if cat in HL:
        # Direct label at the end of each highlighted line — replaces the legend
        ax2.text(df_annual['year'].iloc[-1]+0.05, df_annual[cat].iloc[-1],
                 '  '+cat, color=col, fontsize=9, va='center', fontweight='bold')

# Remove top and right spines — they frame the data but add no information
for spine in ['top','right']:
    ax2.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
# Soften the remaining spines so they recede visually
ax2.spines['left'].set_color('#aaa'); ax2.spines['bottom'].set_color('#aaa')
ax2.tick_params(length=0)  # remove tick marks — the grid already shows scale
ax2.set_title('After — clean, direct labels', fontsize=11)
ax2.set_xticks(df_annual['year'])
ax2.set_xlim(df_annual['year'].min(), df_annual['year'].max()+0.7)
plt.suptitle('Lesson 3 — Eliminate clutter', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

Lesson 4 — Draw Attention Where You Want It¶

All context series → gray. Message series → color.

In [19]:
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))

# Color rule: highlighted products get their full color; all others get gray.
# Gray = context (background information); color = message (what the story is about).
for cat in primary_products:
    col = HL.get(cat, GRAY); lw = 3.0 if cat in HL else 1.2
    ax.plot(df_annual['year'], df_annual[cat], marker='o', color=col, linewidth=lw, zorder=3)
    if cat in HL:
        ax.text(df_annual['year'].iloc[-1]+0.05, df_annual[cat].iloc[-1],
                '  '+cat, color=col, fontsize=10, va='center', fontweight='bold')

# Annotation: point the reader's attention to the key event (COVID 2020 shock)
# xy = the exact data point the arrow tip lands on
# xytext = where the annotation text is placed
ax.annotate('2020: COVID\ncollapses all exports',
            xy=(2020, float(df_annual[df_annual['year']==2020]['Petroleum'])),
            xytext=(2020.2, 6000), fontsize=9, color='#e63946',
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color='#e63946'))

# Remove distracting spines
for spine in ['top','right']:
    ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
ax.set_title('Lesson 4 — Gray = context; color = message', fontsize=12)
ax.set_xticks(df_annual['year'])
ax.set_xlim(df_annual['year'].min(), df_annual['year'].max()+0.8)
ax.set_ylabel('USD millions'); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

/tmp/ipykernel_2299/738479274.py:16: FutureWarning:

Calling float on a single element Series is deprecated and will raise a TypeError in the future. Use float(ser.iloc[0]) instead

Lesson 5 — Think Like a Designer¶

Sentence-style title, single annotation, minimal chrome.

In [20]:
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6.5))

# Same color logic as Lesson 4 — highlighted vs. gray context lines
for cat in primary_products:
    col = HL.get(cat, GRAY); lw = 3.0 if cat in HL else 1.2
    ax.plot(df_annual['year'], df_annual[cat], marker='o', color=col, linewidth=lw, zorder=3)
    if cat in HL:
        ax.text(df_annual['year'].iloc[-1]+0.05, df_annual[cat].iloc[-1],
                '  '+cat, color=col, fontsize=11, va='center', fontweight='bold')

# Sentence-style title — states the conclusion, not just the topic.
# loc='left' aligns it to the left so it reads like a newspaper headline.
ax.set_title("Cocoa Is Ecuador's Fastest-Growing Primary Export\n"
             "— Petroleum Revenue Has Been Falling Since 2022",
             fontsize=13, fontweight='bold', loc='left', color='#1d3557')

# Remove three spines — only keep the bottom axis line
for spine in ['top','right','left']:
    ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
ax.spines['bottom'].set_color('#999'); ax.tick_params(length=0)

# Very faint horizontal grid — helps read values without dominating the chart
ax.grid(axis='y', linestyle='-', alpha=0.12)
ax.set_xticks(df_annual['year'])
ax.set_xlim(df_annual['year'].min(), df_annual['year'].max()+1.0)

# Inline units label instead of a y-axis title — less formal, cleaner
ax.text(df_annual['year'].min(), df_annual['Petroleum'].max()*1.03,
        'Annual exports, USD millions (primary products)', fontsize=9, color='#666')

# Annotation box: quantifies the key insight so the reader doesn't have to calculate
c20 = float(df_annual[df_annual['year']==2020]['Cocoa'])
c24 = float(df_annual[df_annual['year']==2024]['Cocoa'])
ax.annotate(f'+{(c24/c20-1)*100:.0f}% from\n2020 to 2024',
            xy=(2024, c24), xytext=(2021.5, 2500), fontsize=10, color='#6a4c93',
            bbox=dict(boxstyle='round,pad=0.4', fc='#f3eef7', ec='#6a4c93', alpha=0.9),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color='#6a4c93'))
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

/tmp/ipykernel_2299/2850079894.py:32: FutureWarning:

Calling float on a single element Series is deprecated and will raise a TypeError in the future. Use float(ser.iloc[0]) instead

/tmp/ipykernel_2299/2850079894.py:33: FutureWarning:

Calling float on a single element Series is deprecated and will raise a TypeError in the future. Use float(ser.iloc[0]) instead

Lesson 6 — Tell a Story¶

Three sequential panels: setup → complication → resolution.

In [21]:
# Reusable function that draws one "story slide" panel for a given year subset.
# years_sel: list of years to show (controls how much history is revealed)
# hl: dict of {product: color} for highlighted series; everything else → GRAY
# note: optional annotation text placed on the chart
def story_panel(years_sel, hl, title, note=None, note_xy=None, note_txy=None):
    sub = df_annual[df_annual['year'].isin(years_sel)]  # filter to selected years
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 5))
    for cat in primary_products:
        col = hl.get(cat, GRAY); lw = 3.0 if cat in hl else 1.2
        ax.plot(sub['year'], sub[cat], marker='o', color=col, linewidth=lw, zorder=3)
        if cat in hl:
            ax.text(sub['year'].iloc[-1]+0.05, sub[cat].iloc[-1],
                    '  '+cat, color=col, fontsize=10, va='center', fontweight='bold')
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=13, fontweight='bold', loc='left', color='#1d3557')
    for spine in ['top','right','left']:
        ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
    ax.spines['bottom'].set_color('#999'); ax.tick_params(length=0)
    ax.grid(axis='y', linestyle='-', alpha=0.12)
    ax.set_xticks(years_sel)
    ax.set_xlim(min(years_sel)-0.1, max(years_sel)+0.9)
    # Fixed y-axis across all panels so the scale is consistent across "slides"
    ax.set_ylim(0, df_annual[primary_products].max().max()*1.12)
    if note:  # optional annotation pointing to a specific data point
        ax.annotate(note, xy=note_xy, xytext=note_txy, fontsize=10, color='#6a4c93',
                    arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color='#6a4c93'))
    plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

# Panel A — SETUP: show only 2020-2021 to establish petroleum's dominance
story_panel([2020, 2021], {'Petroleum': '#e63946'},
            "1. Petroleum Dominated Ecuador's Primary Exports — Then COVID Hit")

# Panel B — COMPLICATION: extend to 2023 to reveal cocoa's quiet rise
story_panel([2020, 2021, 2022, 2023], {'Petroleum': '#e63946', 'Cocoa': '#6a4c93'},
            "2. Recovery Was Uneven: Cocoa Began a Quiet but Consistent Climb",
            note='Cocoa doubles\nfrom 2020 to 2023',
            note_xy=(2023, float(df_annual[df_annual['year']==2023]['Cocoa'])),
            note_txy=(2021.2, 2500))

# Panel C — RESOLUTION: show the full picture through 2024
story_panel([2020, 2021, 2022, 2023, 2024], {'Petroleum': '#e63946', 'Cocoa': '#6a4c93'},
            "3. By 2024, Cocoa Has Quadrupled — Ecuador's Clearest Diversification Signal")

# Policy recommendation — the explicit call to action that the story argues for
print("=" * 70)
print("POLICY RECOMMENDATION")
print("=" * 70)
print("Ecuador should invest in fine-cacao processing, origin certification,")
print("and direct-trade relationships — capturing more value per kilogram")
print("rather than exporting raw beans at commodity prices.")

/tmp/ipykernel_2299/1764246069.py:36: FutureWarning:

Calling float on a single element Series is deprecated and will raise a TypeError in the future. Use float(ser.iloc[0]) instead


======================================================================
POLICY RECOMMENDATION
======================================================================
Ecuador should invest in fine-cacao processing, origin certification,
and direct-trade relationships — capturing more value per kilogram
rather than exporting raw beans at commodity prices.

Part 6 — Activities¶

Parts 1–5 explored Ecuador's primary exports (PRIMARIOS) — the raw commodities that form the backbone of the country's trade. In these activities you apply the same visualization techniques to Ecuador's industrialized exports (INDUSTRIALIZADOS) — the ten categories of processed and manufactured goods. This lets you practice each technique on a new product group and draw comparisons between the two halves of Ecuador's export economy.

Complete each activity independently using df_monthly (or df_annual where indicated). Each activity is worth the same number of points.

Where a task asks for an interpretation, write your answer in a markdown cell after your code.

🎯 Activity 1: Time Series Decomposition — Industrialized Products¶

In Part 1 we decomposed Petroleum (a primary product). Now apply the same technique to Processed Cocoa (Processed_Cocoa), an industrialized product that directly derives from Ecuador's fastest-growing primary export.

Using df_monthly:

  1. Plot the raw monthly series of Processed_Cocoa as a line chart.
  2. Compute and overlay a 3-month centered rolling mean to approximate the trend.
  3. Compute the deviation from trend (raw − rolling mean) and plot it in a second panel.
  4. In a markdown cell: does Processed Cocoa show a seasonal pattern? How does its pattern compare to raw Cocoa (from Part 1's decomposition example)? What does this tell you about the relationship between the primary and industrialized stages of the cocoa value chain?
In [22]:
pc = df_monthly.set_index('date')['Processed_Cocoa']

# TREND: 3-month centered rolling mean (shorter window than Petroleum's 6-month, appropriate since Processed_Cocoa may have a different noise profile)
pc_trend = pc.rolling(window=3, center=True).mean()

# DEVIATION FROM TREND: raw − rolling mean (seasonality + noise combined, since the activity only asks for a 2-panel view, not a full 4-way split)
pc_deviation = pc - pc_trend

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(13, 6), sharex=True)
fig.suptitle("Activity 1 — Decomposition of Ecuador's Processed Cocoa Exports\n"
             "(Jan 2023 – Oct 2025, real BCE data, 3-month rolling trend)",
             fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')

# Panel 1: raw observed series + trend overlay
axes[0].plot(pc.index, pc.values, color=ind_colors['Processed_Cocoa'],
             linewidth=1.6, marker='o', markersize=3, label='Observed')
axes[0].plot(pc_trend.index, pc_trend.values, color='#e63946',
             linewidth=2, linestyle='--', label='3-mo rolling trend')
axes[0].set_ylabel('USD M')
axes[0].legend(fontsize=9)
axes[0].grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.4)

# Panel 2: deviation from trend
axes[1].plot(pc_deviation.index, pc_deviation.values, color='#9e9e9e', linewidth=1.6)
axes[1].axhline(0, color='black', linewidth=0.5)
axes[1].set_ylabel('Deviation\n(USD M)')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Month')
axes[1].grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.4)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Quick numeric summary to support the interpretation
print(f"Processed Cocoa — mean: {pc.mean():.1f} USD M | std: {pc.std():.1f} USD M | "
      f"coefficient of variation: {pc.std()/pc.mean()*100:.1f}%")

Processed Cocoa — mean: 24.7 USD M | std: 12.9 USD M | coefficient of variation: 52.2%

Processed Cocoa does not show a clear and consistently repeating seasonal pattern. Although the seasonal component fluctuates between approximately −4 and +5 million USD, the peaks and declines do not occur in the same months each year. For example, October 2023 shows a pronounced decline, whereas October 2024 records an increase, and additional peaks appear in March and September 2025. Therefore, the series is characterized more strongly by its upward trend than by seasonality. Monthly export values increased from approximately USD 11 million in January 2023 to nearly USD 50 million at the beginning of 2025.

This pattern is similar to that of raw Cocoa analyzed in Part 1. Both raw and Processed Cocoa exhibit strong growth and considerable short-term fluctuations, but neither presents a stable monthly pattern that repeats clearly across the years. Raw Cocoa also showed one of the largest increases among primary products, with substantial variation across the period. Processed Cocoa appears to follow a broadly similar trajectory, suggesting that the growth of cocoa processing has occurred alongside the expansion of raw cocoa exports.

The similarity between the two series indicates that the primary and industrialized stages of Ecuador’s cocoa value chain are closely connected. As the production and export value of raw Cocoa increase, Processed Cocoa exports also tend to expand, suggesting that the industrial stage is benefiting from the growth of the primary sector rather than evolving independently from it. However, this visual similarity demonstrates association, not causation, and should ideally be confirmed through a direct comparison of growth rates, market shares, and correlations between both series.

Overall, the results suggest that Ecuador is beginning to capture more value through cocoa processing, but the industrialized stage remains considerably smaller than the primary stage. This means that much of the potential value added is still not retained domestically and supports the recommendation to strengthen processing infrastructure, innovation, certification, and access to higher-value international markets.

🎯 Activity 2: Visualization Taxonomies — All 10 Industrialized Products¶

In Part 2 we applied shared-space and split-space taxonomies to primary products. Now apply both to all 10 industrialized products.

Using df_monthly:

  1. Build a shared-space line chart with all 10 industrialized products on one axis. Use ind_colors for the color palette.
  2. Build a split-space small-multiples grid (2×5) with one panel per industrialized product, sharey=True.
  3. In a markdown cell: which taxonomy works better for industrialized products? Is the scale problem (dominant product compressing the others) worse or better than it was for primary products, and why?
In [23]:
# Activity 2.1 — Shared-space: ALL 10 industrialized products on one axis
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(13, 5))
for cat in industrialized_products:
    ax.plot(df_monthly['date'], df_monthly[cat],
            label=cat.replace('_', ' '), color=ind_colors[cat],
            linewidth=1.8, marker='o', markersize=3)

for yr in [2024, 2025]:
    ax.axvline(pd.Timestamp(f'{yr}-01-01'), color='#ccc', linewidth=0.8, linestyle=':')

ax.set_title("Activity 2.1 — Ecuador Monthly Exports — All 10 Industrialized Products (Shared-Space)\n"
             "Jan 2023 – Oct 2025 | Real BCE data", fontsize=12)
ax.set_xlabel('Month'); ax.set_ylabel('Exports (USD millions)')
ax.legend(loc='upper left', fontsize=8, ncol=2)
ax.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.4)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

print("Compare: does one product dominate and compress the others, the way")
print("Petroleum did for primary products in Part 2.1?")

Compare: does one product dominate and compress the others, the way
Petroleum did for primary products in Part 2.1?
In [24]:
# Activity 2.2 — Split-space: ALL 10 industrialized products, 2×5 grid
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 5, figsize=(20, 7), sharey=True)
fig.suptitle("Activity 2.2 — Ecuador Monthly Exports — All 10 Industrialized Products (Split-Space)\n"
             "Jan 2023 – Oct 2025 | Real BCE data", fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')

for ax, cat in zip(axes.flat, industrialized_products):
    ax.fill_between(df_monthly['date'], df_monthly[cat],
                     alpha=0.45, color=ind_colors[cat])
    ax.plot(df_monthly['date'], df_monthly[cat],
            color=ind_colors[cat], linewidth=1.4, marker='o', markersize=2)
    for yr in [2024, 2025]:
        ax.axvline(pd.Timestamp(f'{yr}-01-01'), color='#ccc', linewidth=0.8, linestyle=':')
    ax.set_title(cat.replace('_', ' '), fontsize=9, fontweight='bold')
    ax.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45, labelsize=6)
    ax.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.3)

for row in range(2):
    axes[row, 0].set_ylabel('USD millions')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

🎯 Activity 3: Heatmap — All 10 Industrialized Products¶

In Part 3.1 we built a heatmap comparing Total Primary vs. Total Industrialized. Now drill into the industrialized group itself.

Using df_monthly:

  1. Build a heatmap with rows = the 10 industrialized products, columns = month (Jan–Dec), color = average export value in USD millions. Use the Blues colormap.
  2. Annotate cells with values (annot=True, fmt='.1f').
  3. In a markdown cell: which industrialized products have the strongest seasonal patterns? Compare with the primary product heatmap from Part 3.1 — does the industrialized group show more or less seasonality overall?
In [25]:
month_labels = ['Jan','Feb','Mar','Apr','May','Jun',
                 'Jul','Aug','Sep','Oct','Nov','Dec']

# Pivot: mean value per product per calendar month, transposed so products are rows and months are columns (as the activity specifies)
pivot_ind = df_monthly.groupby('month')[industrialized_products].mean().T
pivot_ind.columns = month_labels
pivot_ind.index = [c.replace('_', ' ') for c in pivot_ind.index]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(13, 6))
sns.heatmap(pivot_ind, cmap='Blues', annot=True, fmt='.1f',
            linewidths=0.4, linecolor='white',
            cbar_kws={'label': 'Average exports (USD millions)'}, ax=ax)
ax.set_title("Activity 3 — Heatmap: All 10 Industrialized Products by Month\n"
             "Rows: Product | Columns: Month (avg across 2023–2025)", fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
ax.set_xlabel('Month'); ax.set_ylabel('Industrialized Product')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Quantify seasonal strength per product: range (max - min month) as a % of that product's own average — a product with no seasonality has a small range relative to its mean; a strongly seasonal one has a large range.
seasonal_strength = ((pivot_ind.max(axis=1) - pivot_ind.min(axis=1)) / pivot_ind.mean(axis=1) * 100).sort_values(ascending=False)
print("Seasonal strength (month-to-month range as % of average) — highest first:")
print(seasonal_strength.round(1))

Seasonal strength (month-to-month range as % of average) — highest first:
Hats                  56.0
Textiles              53.1
Petrol Derivatives    51.9
Processed Coffee      45.2
Fish Meal             36.5
Other Sea Products    35.7
Processed Cocoa       35.0
