Chemicals             33.7
Metal Manufactures    30.2
Other Industrial      20.7
dtype: float64

The industrialized products with the strongest seasonal patterns are Hats, Textiles, and Petroleum Derivatives, with seasonal variation of approximately 56.5%, 52.6%, and 51.8%, respectively. In each case, the highest monthly value is nearly twice the lowest one in relative terms. Petroleum Derivatives show a particularly clear pattern, with a major peak in April and another in September, followed by a pronounced decline in November and December. This resembles the seasonal behavior of crude Petroleum observed earlier, especially the September peak and the year-end decline, which is reasonable because both products belong to different stages of the same value chain. At the opposite extreme, Other Industrialized Products show very weak seasonality, with values remaining relatively stable throughout the year and no clear recurring calendar pattern.

Compared with the primary-product heatmap from Part 3.1, the industrialized group appears less seasonal when viewed only at the aggregate level. However, the product-level analysis reveals a more complex picture. Seven of the ten individual industrialized products display moderate to strong seasonal variation, in some cases comparable to or greater than that of several primary products. The key difference is that their peaks occur in different months. For example, Petroleum Derivatives peak in April and September, Other Sea Products in September and December, and Textiles in May and November.

Because these seasonal peaks are not synchronized, they tend to offset one another when all products are combined into the Total Industrialized series. As a result, the aggregate heatmap appears relatively flat even though many individual components have clear seasonal patterns. This is a case of aggregation cancellation: the absence of visible seasonality in the total does not mean that the individual products lack seasonality, but rather that their cycles occur at different times and partially cancel each other out.

Therefore, the industrialized group shows less seasonality overall at the aggregate level, but substantial seasonality at the individual-product level. This also demonstrates why detailed space-filling views are necessary to complement aggregated heatmaps. Just as split-space revealed patterns hidden in the shared-space chart, the product-level heatmap uncovers seasonal behavior that is obscured when all industrialized products are summed together.

🎯 Activity 4: Interactive Chart — All 10 Industrialized Products¶

Build an interactive Plotly line chart of all 10 industrialized products from df_monthly that:

  1. Uses ind_colors for colors, hover tooltips (product name, date, value in USD M), and hovermode='x unified'.
  2. Adds a range selector (6M, 1Y, All) and a range slider.
  3. In a markdown cell: use the 1Y zoom to focus on 2024. Which industrialized product was most volatile month-to-month? Which showed the most consistent upward trend? How does the volatility of industrialized products compare to what you observed for primary products in Part 4.1?
In [26]:
fig = go.Figure()
for cat in industrialized_products:
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=df_monthly['date'], y=df_monthly[cat],
        mode='lines+markers',
        name=cat.replace('_', ' '),
        line=dict(color=ind_colors[cat], width=1.8),
        marker=dict(size=3),
        hovertemplate='<b>' + cat.replace('_', ' ') + '</b><br>%{x|%b %Y}<br>%{y:,.1f} USD M<extra></extra>'
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    title="Activity 4 — Ecuador Monthly Industrialized Exports — All 10 Products (Interactive)<br>"
          "<sup>Click legend to filter | Drag to zoom | 6M/1Y buttons | Hover for exact values</sup>",
    xaxis_title='Month', yaxis_title='Exports (USD millions)',
    hovermode='x unified',
    height=540,
    legend=dict(x=1.01, y=1),
    xaxis=dict(
        rangeslider=dict(visible=True),
        rangeselector=dict(buttons=[
            dict(count=6, label='6M', step='month', stepmode='backward'),
            dict(count=1, label='1Y', step='year', stepmode='backward'),
            dict(step='all', label='All')])))
fig.show()
In [27]:
# Support code: quantify volatility and trend consistency for 2024 specifically, since the markdown question asks you to zoom into 2024 with the 1Y button.
m24_ind = df_monthly[df_monthly['year'] == 2024]

print("2024 monthly volatility and trend — Industrialized products")
print(f"{'Product':<22}{'CV (%)':>10}{'Trend slope':>15}{'R² (fit)':>12}")
for cat in industrialized_products:
    series = m24_ind[cat].values
    cv = series.std() / series.mean() * 100
    x = np.arange(len(series))
    slope, intercept = np.polyfit(x, series, 1)
    pred = slope * x + intercept
    ss_res = ((series - pred) ** 2).sum()
    ss_tot = ((series - series.mean()) ** 2).sum()
    r2 = 1 - ss_res / ss_tot if ss_tot > 0 else 0
    print(f"{cat.replace('_',' '):<22}{cv:>9.1f}%{slope:>15.2f}{r2:>12.2f}")

2024 monthly volatility and trend — Industrialized products
Product                   CV (%)    Trend slope    R² (fit)
Petrol Derivatives         30.6%          -4.40        0.41
Processed Coffee           16.8%           0.21        0.16
Processed Cocoa            25.6%           1.35        0.67
Fish Meal                  38.0%          -0.76        0.42
Other Sea Products         11.4%           2.91        0.36
Chemicals                   9.7%           0.18        0.14
Metal Manufactures         10.2%           0.41        0.10
Hats                       31.1%          -0.10        0.49
Textiles                   14.7%           0.14        0.23
Other Industrial            9.6%           2.93        0.43

Using the 1Y zoom to focus on 2024, Fish Meal was the most volatile industrialized product on a month-to-month basis, with a coefficient of variation of approximately 38.0%. It was followed by Hats at 31.1% and Petroleum Derivatives at 30.6%. This is not immediately obvious from the interactive chart because Fish Meal operates on a much smaller scale, generally below USD 20 million, while larger categories such as Other Sea Products produce more visually noticeable movements. For this reason, the coefficient of variation is a more appropriate measure than visual inspection alone, since it evaluates volatility relative to each product’s own average.

The industrialized product with the most consistent upward trend was Processed Cocoa. It had a positive monthly slope of approximately 1.35 and the highest coefficient of determination, with an (R^2) of 0.67. This indicates that Processed Cocoa did not simply increase during 2024, but followed a comparatively steady and orderly upward trajectory, with less fluctuation around the trend line. This finding is consistent with the earlier decomposition analysis, which showed that the series was dominated by a strong trend rather than by a stable seasonal pattern.

Compared with the primary products analyzed in Part 4.1, industrialized products were generally less volatile in 2024. Seven of the ten industrialized categories had coefficients of variation below 20%, suggesting a relatively stable month-to-month performance. In the primary group, by contrast, Petroleum showed much more abrupt and unpredictable movements, including the sharp shock observed in July 2025. However, volatility within the industrialized group was not uniform. Petroleum Derivatives also displayed a pronounced decline toward the end of 2024, followed by a strong recovery later in the series, suggesting that some of the instability of crude Petroleum is transmitted to the industrialized stage of the same value chain.

Overall, the results suggest that industrialized products tend to be more stable than primary commodities, but this depends on the type of product. Industrialized goods that remain closely linked to volatile commodities, such as Petroleum Derivatives, can inherit much of the instability of the primary stage. In contrast, more differentiated manufactured products, including Textiles, Chemicals, and Metal Manufactures, appear considerably more stable. This supports the argument that diversification should focus not only on industrialization in general, but specifically on manufacturing activities that generate higher value added and are less exposed to commodity-price volatility.

🎯 Activity 5: Parallel Coordinates — All 10 Industrialized Products¶

In Part 3.6 we built parallel coordinates for all 11 primary products. Now apply the same technique to the 10 industrialized products.

Using df_monthly:

  1. Build a Plotly parallel coordinates chart with all 10 industrialized products as axes. Color by year (2023=teal, 2024=yellow, 2025=red). Use ind_colors values as the colorscale where possible, or use the year-based colorscale from Part 3.6.
  2. In a markdown cell: identify one positive correlation and one negative correlation between industrialized products. Do the correlated pairs make economic sense? Compare the overall structure of this chart with the primary products' parallel coordinates in Part 3.6 — which group shows more correlated co-movement?
In [28]:
fig = px.parallel_coordinates(
    df_monthly,
    dimensions=industrialized_products,
    color='year',
    color_continuous_scale=[[0.0, '#2a9d8f'], [0.5, '#e9c46a'], [1.0, '#e63946']],
    labels={c: c.replace('_', ' ') for c in industrialized_products},
    title='Activity 5 — Parallel Coordinates — All 10 Industrialized Products by Month (2023–2025)'
          '<sup>Each line = one month | Teal=2023 | Yellow=2024 | Red=2025</sup>'
)
fig.update_layout(height=550)
fig.show()

# Correlation highlights to support the written interpretation
print("Correlation highlights (10 industrialized products):")
corr_ind = df_monthly[industrialized_products].corr().round(2)
pairs_ind = [(r, c, corr_ind.loc[r, c]) for r in industrialized_products for c in industrialized_products
             if r < c and (corr_ind.loc[r, c] > 0.5 or corr_ind.loc[r, c] < -0.3)]
pairs_ind.sort(key=lambda x: abs(x[2]), reverse=True)
for r, c, v in pairs_ind[:8]:
    direction = 'positive' if v > 0 else 'negative'
    print(f"  {r} ↔ {c}: {v:+.2f} ({direction})")

# Also compute overall "co-movement strength" for comparison with primary products: average absolute correlation across all pairs, excluding self-correlation.
avg_abs_corr_ind = corr_ind.where(~np.eye(len(industrialized_products), dtype=bool)).abs().mean().mean()
corr_prim = df_monthly[primary_products].corr()
avg_abs_corr_prim = corr_prim.where(~np.eye(len(primary_products), dtype=bool)).abs().mean().mean()
print(f"\nAverage |correlation| — Industrialized: {avg_abs_corr_ind:.2f} | Primary: {avg_abs_corr_prim:.2f}")

Correlation highlights (10 industrialized products):
  Other_Sea_Products ↔ Processed_Cocoa: +0.81 (positive)
  Chemicals ↔ Other_Sea_Products: +0.72 (positive)
  Other_Industrial ↔ Other_Sea_Products: +0.66 (positive)
  Other_Industrial ↔ Processed_Cocoa: +0.65 (positive)
  Chemicals ↔ Other_Industrial: +0.61 (positive)
  Petrol_Derivatives ↔ Processed_Cocoa: -0.60 (negative)
  Chemicals ↔ Processed_Cocoa: +0.58 (positive)
  Other_Sea_Products ↔ Petrol_Derivatives: -0.48 (negative)

Average |correlation| — Industrialized: 0.30 | Primary: 0.17

A clear positive correlation appears between Other Sea Products and Processed Cocoa, with a coefficient of approximately +0.81, the strongest positive relationship in the industrialized group. However, this pair does not have an obvious direct productive link, since processed seafood and processed cocoa do not share the same inputs, harvesting cycle, or value chain. The relationship is more likely explained by a common upward trend: both categories increased steadily between 2023 and 2025, so they move together over time even though one does not necessarily cause the other to grow. A similar interpretation applies to other positive pairs, such as Chemicals and Other Sea Products.

The strongest negative correlation is between Petroleum Derivatives and Processed Cocoa, at approximately −0.60. This relationship is economically interpretable, but not because the two products directly compete. Rather, they followed opposite trajectories during the period: Processed Cocoa grew consistently, while Petroleum Derivatives declined and experienced a sharp fall toward the end of 2024. Their negative correlation therefore reflects two contrasting export trends occurring at the same time—expansion in the cocoa value chain and contraction in petroleum-based industrial exports.

The correlated pairs make partial economic sense, but they should not automatically be interpreted as causal relationships. The positive correlations are likely driven mainly by shared trends and common macroeconomic conditions rather than direct production links. In contrast, the negative relationship involving Petroleum Derivatives is meaningful as a description of structural divergence: one industrialized sector was expanding while another was losing momentum. Correlation in this chart should therefore be understood as co-movement, not proof that changes in one product directly cause changes in another.

Compared with the parallel-coordinates chart for primary products in Part 3.6, the industrialized group shows stronger overall co-movement. The average absolute correlation is approximately 0.30 for industrialized products, compared with about 0.17 for primary products. This difference is economically plausible because primary commodities such as Petroleum, Bananas, Shrimp, Cocoa, and Coffee are affected by distinct harvest cycles, international prices, climatic conditions, and supply shocks. Industrialized products, by contrast, may respond more similarly to common factors such as manufacturing activity, investment, export demand, logistics, and conditions in shared destination markets. As a result, their lines tend to move together more frequently, even when there is no direct productive connection between the individual products.

🎯 Activity 6: Storytelling — Build a Narrative About Industrialized Exports¶

Apply Knaflic's six lessons (demonstrated in Part 5 using primary products) to build a persuasive chart about one or two industrialized products.

  1. Write a one-sentence Big Idea (audience + message + call to action) in a markdown cell.
  2. Choose one or two industrialized products that tell an interesting story. Build a single polished chart using df_annual (for the 2020–2024 arc) applying all six lessons:
    • Line chart (appropriate display for trends)
    • Decluttered (no border spines, no legend — use direct labels)
    • Focused attention (your chosen product(s) in color, rest in #cccccc(gray color))
    • Sentence-style title stating the conclusion
    • One annotation naming the key quantitative insight
  3. In a markdown cell: what policy action does your chart argue for? How does the story of your chosen industrialized product complement or contrast with the cocoa narrative from Part 5?
In [29]:
# Activity 6: Storytelling — Industrialized exports
# Big idea: Ecuador should invest in cocoa processing because Processed_Cocoa is the clearest value-added industrial export story.
# Chemicals is shown as a secondary industrial benchmark: steady growth, but a less dramatic narrative than cocoa.

GRAY = '#cccccc'
HL = {
    'Processed_Cocoa': '#4a1942',
    'Chemicals': '#f4a261',
}

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6.5))

for product in industrialized_products:
    color = HL.get(product, GRAY)
    linewidth = 3.0 if product in HL else 1.2
    ax.plot(
        df_annual['year'],
        df_annual[product],
        marker='o',
        color=color,
        linewidth=linewidth,
        zorder=3,
    )
    if product in HL:
        ax.text(
            df_annual['year'].iloc[-1] + 0.06,
            df_annual[product].iloc[-1],
            '  ' + product.replace('_', ' '),
            color=color,
            fontsize=10,
            va='center',
            fontweight='bold',
        )

# Quantify the key point so the chart tells a concrete story.
pc_2020 = float(df_annual.loc[df_annual['year'] == 2020, 'Processed_Cocoa'].iloc[0])
pc_2024 = float(df_annual.loc[df_annual['year'] == 2024, 'Processed_Cocoa'].iloc[0])
chem_2020 = float(df_annual.loc[df_annual['year'] == 2020, 'Chemicals'].iloc[0])
chem_2024 = float(df_annual.loc[df_annual['year'] == 2024, 'Chemicals'].iloc[0])

ax.annotate(
    f'Processed Cocoa +{(pc_2024 / pc_2020 - 1) * 100:.0f}% from 2020 to 2024',
    xy=(2024, pc_2024),
    xytext=(2021.35, pc_2024 * 1.35),
    fontsize=10,
    color='#4a1942',
    bbox=dict(boxstyle='round,pad=0.4', fc='#f3eef7', ec='#4a1942', alpha=0.9),
    arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color='#4a1942'),
)

ax.annotate(
    f'Chemicals +{(chem_2024 / chem_2020 - 1) * 100:.0f}% from 2020 to 2024',
    xy=(2024, chem_2024),
    xytext=(2021.45, chem_2024 * 2.8),
    fontsize=9,
    color='#b36b2c',
    bbox=dict(boxstyle='round,pad=0.35', fc='#fff4e8', ec='#b36b2c', alpha=0.9),
    arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color='#b36b2c'),
)

for spine in ['top', 'right', 'left']:
    ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
ax.spines['bottom'].set_color('#999')
ax.tick_params(length=0)
ax.grid(axis='y', linestyle='-', alpha=0.12)
ax.set_xticks(df_annual['year'])
ax.set_xlim(df_annual['year'].min() - 0.1, df_annual['year'].max() + 1.0)
ax.set_ylim(0, df_annual[industrialized_products].max().max() * 1.15)

ax.set_title(
    "Processed Cocoa Is Ecuador's Clearest Industrial Value-Added Opportunity\n"
    "— Chemicals Provides a Steady But Smaller Industrial Growth Signal",
    fontsize=13,
    fontweight='bold',
    loc='left',
    color='#1d3557',
)
ax.text(
    df_annual['year'].min(),
    df_annual[industrialized_products].max().max() * 1.08,
    'Annual exports, USD millions (industrialized products)',
    fontsize=9,
    color='#666',
)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print('Processed Cocoa annual values:')
print(df_annual[['year', 'Processed_Cocoa']].to_string(index=False))
print('\nChemicals annual values:')
print(df_annual[['year', 'Chemicals']].to_string(index=False))

Processed Cocoa annual values:
 year  Processed_Cocoa
 2020       119.565458
 2021       122.823278
 2022       139.899225
 2023       152.006223
 2024       266.739928

Chemicals annual values:
 year  Chemicals
 2020 123.611429
 2021 166.867546
 2022 206.832374
 2023 170.325504
 2024 205.511032

Activity 6 — Interpretation¶

The chart shows a clear value-added story: Processed Cocoa rises from about 119.6 USD M in 2020 to 266.7 USD M in 2024, a gain of roughly 123%. That makes it the strongest industrialized export signal in the section, and it directly complements the cocoa narrative from Part 5 by showing that the growth story is not only in raw cocoa, but also in the industrial stage that captures more value.

Chemicals also grows over the period, but the pattern is steadier and less dramatic, reaching about 205.5 USD M in 2024 after a dip in 2023. The policy implication is to prioritize cocoa processing, certification, and supply-chain investment, while continuing to support broader industrial capacity. In other words, Ecuador's clearest industrial opportunity is to move more of the cocoa story from commodity exports into processed exports with higher value per unit.

🎯 Activity 7: Brushing + Linking — Industrialized Products¶

In section 4.3 we built a brushing + linking chart for three primary products (Petroleum, Shrimp, Cocoa). Now apply the same technique to three industrialized products of your choice.

Tasks:

  1. Choose three industrialized products from industrialized_products — pick ones you expect to behave differently from each other.
  2. Build a 3-panel brushing + linking chart using the same structure as section 4.3: shared x-axis, rangeslider as the brush, year-colored dots.
  3. Brush over at least two different time windows and describe what you observe in each panel.
  4. Write one insight that was only visible after brushing — something you could not see in the full-range view.

Hint: The industrialized products are stored in df_monthly with the same column structure as the primary ones. Check industrialized_products for the full list.

In [30]:
# Activity 7: Brushing + Linking for industrialized products
# Chosen products: Petrol_Derivatives, Chemicals, and Textiles.
# They differ in level, volatility, and trend, which makes the linked view more informative.

import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots

chosen_products = ['Petrol_Derivatives', 'Chemicals', 'Textiles']
chosen_colors = {
    'Petrol_Derivatives': '#264653',
    'Chemicals': '#f4a261',
    'Textiles': '#94d2bd',
}

# Quick summary to support the written interpretation after brushing.
print('Chosen industrialized products:')
for product in chosen_products:
    cv = df_monthly[product].std() / df_monthly[product].mean() * 100
    yearly_means = df_monthly.groupby('year')[product].mean().round(1)
    print(f"  {product.replace('_', ' '):<20} CV={cv:.1f}% | yearly means: {yearly_means.to_dict()}")

correlation_matrix = df_monthly[chosen_products].corr().round(2)
print('\nPairwise correlations:')
for i, left in enumerate(chosen_products):
    for right in chosen_products[i + 1:]:
        print(f"  {left.replace('_', ' ')} vs {right.replace('_', ' ')}: {correlation_matrix.loc[left, right]:+.2f}")

# Shared-x brushing and linking structure, mirroring the primary-products version.
dates = df_monthly['date']
year_color = df_monthly['date'].dt.year.map({2023: '#5e4fa2', 2024: '#3288bd', 2025: '#d53e4f'})

fig = make_subplots(
    rows=3,
    cols=1,
    shared_xaxes=True,
    subplot_titles=[
        'Petrol Derivatives (USD M) — brush with the rangeslider below',
        'Chemicals (USD M) — linked view',
        'Textiles (USD M) — linked view',
    ],
    vertical_spacing=0.08,
    row_heights=[0.38, 0.31, 0.31],
)

for row, product in enumerate(chosen_products, start=1):
    fig.add_trace(
        go.Scatter(
            x=dates,
            y=df_monthly[product],
            mode='lines',
            line=dict(color=chosen_colors[product], width=1.5),
            showlegend=False,
            hoverinfo='skip',
        ),
        row=row,
        col=1,
    )
    fig.add_trace(
        go.Scatter(
            x=dates,
            y=df_monthly[product],
            mode='markers',
            marker=dict(color=year_color, size=8, line=dict(width=1, color='white')),
            name=product.replace('_', ' '),
            customdata=df_monthly['date'].dt.strftime('%b %Y'),
            hovertemplate='<b>' + product.replace('_', ' ') + '</b><br>%{customdata}<br>%{y:,.0f} USD M<extra></extra>',
        ),
        row=row,
        col=1,
    )

for yr, col in [('2023', '#5e4fa2'), ('2024', '#3288bd'), ('2025', '#d53e4f')]:
    fig.add_trace(
        go.Scatter(
            x=[None],
            y=[None],
            mode='markers',
            marker=dict(color=col, size=10),
            name=yr,
            showlegend=True,
        )
    )

fig.update_xaxes(
    rangeslider=dict(visible=True, thickness=0.06),
    rangeselector=dict(buttons=[
        dict(count=6, label='6M', step='month', stepmode='backward'),
        dict(count=12, label='1Y', step='month', stepmode='backward'),
        dict(step='all', label='All'),
    ]),
    row=3,
    col=1,
)

fig.update_layout(
    title=dict(
        text='Brushing + Linking — Industrialized Products<br>'
             '<sup>Drag the rangeslider to brush a time window; all three panels stay aligned</sup>',
        font=dict(size=15),
    ),
    height=680,
    hovermode='x unified',
    legend=dict(title='Year', orientation='h', x=0.01, y=1.03),
    plot_bgcolor='#f8f9fa',
    paper_bgcolor='white',
)

fig.update_yaxes(showgrid=True, gridcolor='#e0e0e0')
fig.show()

print('\nHow to brush:')
print('  1. Use the rangeslider at the bottom to select a time window.')
print('  2. Compare the same months across all three products simultaneously.')
print('  3. Try a 2024 window to compare steady growth vs. volatility.')

Chosen industrialized products:
  Petrol Derivatives   CV=37.6% | yearly means: {2023: 94.0, 2024: 77.1, 2025: 57.3}
  Chemicals            CV=15.2% | yearly means: {2023: 14.2, 2024: 17.1, 2025: 18.1}
  Textiles             CV=16.8% | yearly means: {2023: 5.8, 2024: 6.8, 2025: 6.0}

Pairwise correlations:
  Petrol Derivatives vs Chemicals: -0.39
  Petrol Derivatives vs Textiles: -0.07
  Chemicals vs Textiles: +0.34

How to brush:
  1. Use the rangeslider at the bottom to select a time window.
  2. Compare the same months across all three products simultaneously.
  3. Try a 2024 window to compare steady growth vs. volatility.

Activity 7 — Interpretation¶

I brushed over two useful windows: the full 2023–2025 range and a narrower 2024/2025 focus. The pattern is easy to read once the chart is linked across panels: Petrol Derivatives is the most volatile series by far, with a much higher coefficient of variation than the other two products and a clear decline in its yearly average from 2023 to 2025. Chemicals shows the cleanest upward trend, while Textiles grows more modestly and stays comparatively stable.

The pairwise correlations support the visual story: Chemicals and Textiles move together moderately, while Petrol Derivatives moves weakly or even in the opposite direction relative to Chemicals. The insight that became visible only after brushing was that the 2025 window contains a sharp collapse and rebound in Petrol Derivatives, while Chemicals keeps climbing through the same period. That contrast is hard to notice in the full-range view, but it becomes obvious when the time window is narrowed.

Overall, the industrialized group looks less seasonal and more stable than the primary products from Part 4.3, but there is still meaningful heterogeneity. Among these three products, the value-added story is strongest in Chemicals: steady growth, limited volatility, and a trajectory that is easier to plan around than the more erratic fuel-related series.

Summary¶

In this lab you worked with the complete BCE export dataset — all 11 primary and 10 industrialized products, 34 months of real monthly data (Jan 2023 – Oct 2025) — to practice the Week 2 toolbox:

Concept Technique Products used
Time series components Decomposition (6-mo rolling mean) Petroleum (monthly)
Shared-space taxonomy Line chart — top 5 primary 5 primary
Split-space taxonomy Small multiples 3×4 grid All 11 primary
Space-filling — heatmap Primary vs. Industrialized by month 2 aggregates
Space-filling — calendar Daily synthetic from monthly Total Exports
Stacked / stream graphs Primary vs. Industrialized % share 2 aggregates
Scatter + box plots Primary vs. Industrialized groups 2 aggregates
Sankey diagrams Top 5 primary → destination markets 5 primary
Parallel coordinates All 11 primary profiles by year 11 primary
Interactive — line chart All 11 primary products 11 primary
Interactive — dashboard Overview, detail, seasonality, growth All + aggregates
Storytelling Knaflic's 6 lessons → Cocoa narrative Primary (annual)
Activity 2 Primary vs. Industrialized mix All 21 products
Activity 5 Parallel coords — industrialized 10 industrialized
Activity 6 Value-added narrative Aggregates (annual)

The two central lessons: match the visualization to the question (split-space for many products, heatmap for seasonality, Sankey for flows), and design with intent when explaining — eliminate clutter, focus attention, and sequence visuals into a story that drives a decision.
